EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of wintry precipitation impacted portions of eastern New York on the 6th and 7th. The first was a round of light to moderate snow after midnight on 6th to around sunrise, which resulted in one to five inches of snow along and north of the I-88/I-90 corridor. Then, periods of freezing rain occurred from the afternoon of the 6th into the morning of the 7th mainly along and north of I-90. Reports of a quarter to a half an inch of ice accretion were received in the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks into Washington County. The freezing rain quickly turned to heavy snow during the afternoon of the 7th as a strong low pressure system passed south of the area. This round of snow resulted in a quick 6-12 inches of accumulation in the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Lake George Saratoga Region, with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour were common. In the wake of the snowfall, westerly winds gusted to 40-55 miles per hour in the afternoon and evening of the 7th, highest south of I-90.||The weight of the heavy snow and ice on trees along with strong winds resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines. Nearly 90,000 customers lost power, with Saratoga (46,000) and Warren (22,000) Counties being hit the hardest. Over half of Warren County was without power, prompting warming shelters to be opened. Several accidents occurred due to the snowy and icy roads across the southern Adirondacks and Lake George Saratoga Region, including one fatality in Mayfield, and major damage to a fire engine in Chester. A man in Lake Luzerne was injured when he was pierced in the leg while driving a vehicle, and a Thurman Highway Department truck was damaged when a tree fell onto it. Travel was discouraged due to the conditions in Saratoga Springs.
A strong cold front passed and deep low pressure tracked to the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station in Orient reported a 56 mph wind gust at 1001 am. A trained spotter in Orient measured a 50 mph wind gust at 544 am. The Fishers Island Airport measured a 51 mph wind gust around 9 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The 2nd longest heatwave in modern history (1900-onward) occurred across portions of NY and VT from June 18th through June 23rd. Temperatures exceeded 90 degrees for up to 6 consecutive days in portions of the St. Lawrence and Champlain Valleys. Daily High temperature records were set at Massena on June 22nd and June 23rd with high temperatures of 93 and 92 degrees respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in the upper 80s to lower 90s for many parts of the St. Lawrence Valley for six days with increasing humidity on the tail end of this stretch, bringing heat indices into the mid 90s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An mid-atmospheric impulse moved across a marginally unstable air mass across Ontario and southern St. Lawrence county during the late afternoon/evening of June 27th. Much of the thunderstorm activity diminished across Ontario province, but one thunderstorm briefly intensified across southern St. Lawrence county producing some localized damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines downed by thunderstorm winds on the north and east sides of Gouverneur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture laden storm system tracked along a old cold front from the the Gulf of Mexico up across NY and VT. On Saturday, January 11th, the front was positioned across southern Canada with temperatures in the 50s/60s across NY. As the front slowly progressed south into northern NY during the evening, moderate to heavy rain fell across the region. Shortly after midnight of January 12th, the cold front slipped south of the Adirondacks with rain changing to freezing rain across the St. Lawrence and northern Champlain Valleys. ||Widespread 1/4 to 1/2 inches of ice accretion with locally up to 3/4 inch was observed along the international border, resulting in hazardous travel and scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretion  reports of 1/4 to 1/2 inch with locally up to 3/4 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual 3 to 4 foot southeast to south swell on the ocean waters after the passage of low pressure to the south and east helped prevent water drainage from the Long Island south shore back bays. This in combination with unusually high astronomical tides associated with the full moon of the 7th led to isolated moderate coastal flooding along the south shore back bays of Nassau County during the morning high tide cycles of the 7th and 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The moderate coastal flood threshold of 6.2 ft MLLW was touched at the USGS tidal gauge at Hudson Bay at Freeport from 2020-05-08 20:54 EST to 2020-05-08 21:12 EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across Long Island and portions of New York City during the early morning hours in association with a surface trough south of the area. With weak steering flow and high moisture content (precipitable water values 2+ on the Upton, NY sounding), this resulted in flash flooding across portions of Nassau and Suffolk Counties. While rainfall totals averaged 1-2 inches across New York City and Long Island, portions of southern Nassau County and southwestern Suffolk County received 3-5 inches of rain. Reports from automated weather observing stations include 3.34 in West Babylon, 3.39 in Freeport, 4.25 in West Gilgo Beach, and 4.46 in Merrick. CoCoRaHS observers reported 3.69 one mile NNE of Wantagh, 4.05 ENE of Copiague, and 5.41 one mile SSW of Massapequa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park Avenue was closed at Oakland Avenue in Wantagh due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northeast gales associated with low pressure passing to the south, and high astronomical tides associated with the full moon of the 31st, combined to cause isolated moderate coastal flooding along the shorelines of southern Nassau County during the morning high tide cycle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak water level of 6.4 ft MLLW occurred at the USGS tidal gauge at Hudson Bay at Freeport at 2020-10-30 07:30 EST. The moderate coastal flood threshold of 6.2 ft MLLW at Freeport was exceeded from 2020-10-30 07:00 EST to 2020-10-30 08:06 EST.||Also, a peak water level of 6.7 ft MLLW occurred at the USGS tidal gauge at Reynolds Channel from 2020-10-30 07:12 EST to 2020-10-30 07:54 EST, just shy of the moderate coastal flood threshold of 6.8 ft MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along the Middle Atlantic coast on Wednesday December 16, 2020. The low deepened as it tracked northward towards Long Island, NY through the night on December 16, 2020.  Arctic high pressure situated over southeast Canada provided a cold and dry air mass ahead of the low supporting moderate to locally heavy snowfall at the onset. The low pressure ultimately tracked from near Atlantic City, NJ to about 50 miles south of Fire Island, NY before sliding offshore Thursday December 17, 2020. This track led to warmer air aloft moving over the NYC metro and Long Island, changing the snow to a mixture of sleet and rain. Surface temperatures briefly warmed into the upper 30s across central and eastern Long Island early on December 17, 2020, changing the precipitation to plain rain. Once the low began moving away from Long Island, temperatures fell significantly below freezing with snow returning as the event ended. Any snowmelt and standing water refroze on Long Island. Any mixed precipitation was brief in the NYC metro and predominately snow fell across the Lower Hudson Valley. The storm significantly impacted travel during the evening commute on Wednesday December 16, 2020 and the morning commute on Thursday December 17, 2020. Many accidents resulted from the snow and icy conditions. Snowfall rates ranged from 1 to 2 inches per hour Wednesday evening. Total snowfall ranged from around 4 inches across coastal southeast Long Island to as much as 14 inches in Orange County.  6 to 12 inches of snow fell in the NYC metro. Strong wind gusts also occurred with the storm, 35 to 60 mph, with some coastal stations reporting gusts as high as 74 mph on Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer 2 miles east of Yorktown Heights measured 11 inches of snow and a CoCORaHS observer 2 miles south of Peach Lake measured 10.9 inches of snow. Amateur radio and trained spotters measured 10 to 12 inches of snow. Winds gusted to 40 mph at the Westchester County Airport at 6:56 am on December 17, 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet and windy storm moved across Canada and NY on Christmas Eve and Christmas. Temperatures were in the 50s, melting any existing snowpack and rainfall was 1 to 2 inches across much of the region with localized 3 inches in portions of Essex county. The combination of rainfall and snow melt cause many streams and rivers to rise substantially and this included the AuSable River to flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads witnessed flooding due to flooded mountain streams and smaller rivers caused by heavy rainfall and snow melt. Some of the communities impacted included; Elizabethtown, Jay, Keene, Lewis, Minerva, Moriah, North Hudson and Westport.

Several inches of snow occurred in the western Adirondacks ahead of a strengthening low pressure system from the late evening of the 26th into the morning of the 27th. As the low continued to strengthen north of the area, cold air poured in and windy conditions developed. This allowed a lake effect snow band to form and persist from the morning of the 27th into the afternoon of the 28th. Localized blizzard conditions occurred within this band over northern Herkimer County. The main snow band moved north of the area during the afternoon of the 28th, but returned during the evening into the overnight hours, with a few more additional inches of snow. Total accumulation from the 26th through the 29th ranged from a couple of inches over southern Hamilton County to a foot and a half over northern Herkimer and northern Hamilton Counties.

Several inches of snow occurred in the western Adirondacks ahead of a strengthening low pressure system from the late evening of the 26th into the morning of the 27th. As the low continued to strengthen north of the area, cold air poured in and windy conditions developed. This allowed a lake effect snow band to form and persist from the morning of the 27th into the afternoon of the 28th. Localized blizzard conditions occurred within this band over northern Herkimer County. The main snow band moved north of the area during the afternoon of the 28th, but returned during the evening into the overnight hours, with a few more additional inches of snow. Total accumulation from the 26th through the 29th ranged from a couple of inches over southern Hamilton County to a foot and a half over northern Herkimer and northern Hamilton Counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 3 to 5 inches in northern Essex with 4 to 7 inches in southern Essex county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meso-low enhanced snowfall over a small region on the Tug Hill and eastward through the Black River Valley behind an Arctic cold front. As the lake effect band wavered in post-frontal flow, the effect of the meso-low made the band hang up almost directly over the Lowville for a short time midday on January 8. With impressive reflectivities on radar, this resulted in a small around near Lowville receiving 12 of snow in a short time.

Strengthening low pressure moved across Ontario into Quebec province during the afternoon of March 20th. This storm developed a strong low-level jet (5000-6000 ft) of 50-60+ knots across the region. In addition, a strong cold front moved across an unseasonably mild air mass to develop scattered showers and low-topped thunderstorms.|The updrafts of some of these thunderstorms reached vertically enough to tap into the low-level jet allowing for isolated damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Convective winds downed several trees, snapped a few and damaged a wooden shed.

Lesser snowfall totals were measured across northern NY, northern VT and southern VT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall observations of 5 to 8 inches across southeast St. Lawrence, southern Franklin and western Essex counties with minimal impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the Great Lakes pulled an occluded front across Central New York and the Finger Lakes during the late afternoon and early evening of the 29th. This sparked some scattered showers and thunderstorms. One of these storms produced nickel sized hail in the Merrifield area of Cayuga County.

A late season snowfall impacted the region on Monday, March 23rd, 2020. This was brought on by an anomalously cold air mass interacting with a coastal low pressure system. Snowfall totals ranged from an inch in the Mid-Hudson valley up to almost 10 inches in the southern Adirondacks and northern Lake George Saratoga region. The lower amounts were mainly confined to the Lower Hudson Valley due to a rain and snow mix throughout much of the day. Snow intensity increased during the late afternoon and early evening hours impacting the evening commute. As a result, a tractor trailer crashed on Interstate 87 South prompting portions of the interstate to be closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening surface low tracked from Georgian Bay to southern Quebec, slowly deepening with time. A trailing cold front swept east across the eastern Great Lakes. All of this was driven phasing short waves in the northern and southern streams. While the track of the low fit the climatology for high wind events in our area, the thermodynamics were atypical. The strong winds aloft and best wind potential were primarily in the warm sector. By the time strong cold advection and pressure rises arrived, wind speeds aloft weakened substantially. The steepening of lapse rates in the warm sector coincided with an advancing mid-level dry slot, allowing for the best wind to mix down in the warm sector. The window of strong winds was very brief. Coincidentally, the warm sector destabilized enough that when it reached the eastern portions of the area, high shear, low CAPE, thunderstorms developed east of the Finger Lakes. This produced localized damage in Oswego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down on Reservoir Road.

Strengthening low pressure moved across Ontario into Quebec province during the afternoon of March 20th. This storm developed a strong low-level jet (5000-6000 ft) of 50-60+ knots across the region. In addition, a strong cold front moved across an unseasonably mild air mass to develop scattered showers and low-topped thunderstorms.|The updrafts of some of these thunderstorms reached vertically enough to tap into the low-level jet allowing for isolated damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Convective winds downed trees onto a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening surface low tracked from Georgian Bay to southern Quebec, slowly deepening with time. A trailing cold front swept east across the eastern Great Lakes. All of this was driven phasing short waves in the northern and southern streams. While the track of the low fit the climatology for high wind events in our area, the thermodynamics were atypical. The strong winds aloft and best wind potential were primarily in the warm sector. By the time strong cold advection and pressure rises arrived, wind speeds aloft weakened substantially. The steepening of lapse rates in the warm sector coincided with an advancing mid-level dry slot, allowing for the best wind to mix down in the warm sector. The window of strong winds was very brief. Coincidentally, the warm sector destabilized enough that when it reached the eastern portions of the area, high shear, low CAPE, thunderstorms developed east of the Finger Lakes. This produced localized damage in Oswego County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, nearly vertically stacked low moved into the western Great Lakes. Downstream of the low, a warm front moved northeast across the area during the morning, followed by a pre-frontal trough which entered Western NY early in the afternoon, then reached the western Finger Lakes by late afternoon. A narrow plume of modestly unstable air developed in the warm sector between the warm front and pre-frontal trough as temperatures briefly warmed into the upper 60s to lower 70s and surface dewpoints rose into the upper 50s to around 60. Very strong deep layer shear was also present. A broken line of convection developed over Lake Erie by midday and advanced east across Western NY with several different line segments and brief strong cores developing. The most impressive line segment moved right across the Buffalo Metro area during the start of the event with a measured 68 mph wind gust at the Buffalo-Niagara International Airport. This line segment continued east-northeast to the western suburbs of Rochester before rapidly weakening. Other brief line segments and elevated cores developed south of Rochester with hail and gusty winds. All the storms rapidly weakened and dissipated as they moved towards central New York, where the boundary layer did not destabilize due to lingering low clouds.

A developing area of low pressure moved along the northeast coast during the daytime hours on March 23rd. Light snow developed during the predawn hours on the 23rd. There was a brief lull in the snow during the mid to late morning hours. Then, as the coastal low rapidly intensified, a band of heavy snow developed over the area during the afternoon and early evening hours. Oneida County was hardest hit by this winter system. Snow totals there ranged from 6 to 10 inches, except lower amounts of 3 to 6 inches in the immediate Mohawk Valley around Utica. The snow quickly tapered off and ended by late evening in most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed in the wake of a cold front. These strong winds resulted in a roof getting ripped off of a barn in Herkimer county. Wind gusts reports of 57 mph were also recorded in Montgomery county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn roof was ripped off due to strong winds near the town of Schuyler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fire on Breakneck Ridge began early afternoon on Monday, March 9th near the town of Cold Spring, NY. By the morning of March 10th, 150 acres of land had burned. Several agencies responded to help fight the fire, including fire departments from Dutchess Junction, North Highlands, Garrison, Putnam Station, Rombout, Cold Spring and Continental Village. Additionally, Cold Spring police and forest rangers from the state parks and Department of Environmental Conservation aided in putting out the fire. The fire was contained by Tuesday afternoon, March 10th. Eleven cars parked at the Breakneck parking lot were burned with no injuries reported. On March 18th, the state Department of Conservation said the fire had burned 277 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: It was reported that a Metro-North diesel work train locomotive at the brush fire location between Cold Spring and Beacon experienced a malfunction at the beginning of the incident. The fire jumped a road and spread up the hillside toward Breakneck Ridge. The fire continued overnight and was out by Tuesday afternoon. No injuries were reported but 11 cars in the Breakneck Ridge parking lot were burned along with $6,000 worth of the parks supplies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow occurred in the western Adirondacks ahead of a strengthening low pressure system from the late evening of the 26th into the morning of the 27th. As the low continued to strengthen north of the area, cold air poured in and windy conditions developed. This allowed a lake effect snow band to form and persist from the morning of the 27th into the afternoon of the 28th. Localized blizzard conditions occurred within this band over northern Herkimer County. The main snow band moved north of the area during the afternoon of the 28th, but returned during the evening into the overnight hours, with a few more additional inches of snow. Total accumulation from the 26th through the 29th ranged from a couple of inches over southern Hamilton County to a foot and a half over northern Herkimer and northern Hamilton Counties.

Record May Heat produced the instability and the mountains of the Adirondacks produced the lift to develop thunderstorms in the afternoon that very slowly moved south to north along the mountain ridges. A few storms produced wet micro-bursts that caused some tree and utility line damage. ||In addition, Daily high temperatures of 93 and 95 degrees broke daily records for Saranac Lake and Massena respectively. The 93 degrees a Saranac Lake was the 2nd warmest temperature observed in May since 1911 and the 95 degrees at Massena was the 2nd warmest observed in May since 1949.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on a car between Routes 13 and 11. Multiple trees down along Route 11 north of Irona.

A late season snowfall impacted the region on Monday, March 23rd, 2020. This was brought on by an anomalously cold air mass interacting with a coastal low pressure system. Snowfall totals ranged from an inch in the Mid-Hudson valley up to almost 10 inches in the southern Adirondacks and northern Lake George Saratoga region. The lower amounts were mainly confined to the Lower Hudson Valley due to a rain and snow mix throughout much of the day. Snow intensity increased during the late afternoon and early evening hours impacting the evening commute. As a result, a tractor trailer crashed on Interstate 87 South prompting portions of the interstate to be closed.

A deepening surface low tracked from Georgian Bay to southern Quebec, slowly deepening with time. A trailing cold front swept east across the eastern Great Lakes. All of this was driven phasing short waves in the northern and southern streams. While the track of the low fit the climatology for high wind events in our area, the thermodynamics were atypical. The strong winds aloft and best wind potential were primarily in the warm sector. By the time strong cold advection and pressure rises arrived, wind speeds aloft weakened substantially. The steepening of lapse rates in the warm sector coincided with an advancing mid-level dry slot, allowing for the best wind to mix down in the warm sector. The window of strong winds was very brief. Coincidentally, the warm sector destabilized enough that when it reached the eastern portions of the area, high shear, low CAPE, thunderstorms developed east of the Finger Lakes. This produced localized damage in Oswego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on Route 78.

A warm frontal boundary moved through the area during the afternoon hours of Friday, March 20th, 2020. Many thunderstorms developed as the front pushed through the region but most storms remained sub-severe with only two reports of trees and power lines down in Herkimer county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed, resulting in power outages in the town of Old Forge.

A surface low tracked across the southern Adirondacks and central Vermont during the afternoon and early evening hours of May 15th. South of the storm track (Rutland-Windsor counties southward) areas got into the warm sector, thus marginally to moderately unstable. Along and north of the track, there were only pockets of instability due to occasional breaks in cloud cover. A strong mid-level jet max moved along the boundary, thus creating widespread severe wind damage south of the track, especially extreme southern VT, NH, NY, and MA. A few isolated wind damage reports occurred north of the track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees downed by thunderstorms winds in Ticonderoga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow occurred in the western Adirondacks ahead of a strengthening low pressure system from the late evening of the 26th into the morning of the 27th. As the low continued to strengthen north of the area, cold air poured in and windy conditions developed. This allowed a lake effect snow band to form and persist from the morning of the 27th into the afternoon of the 28th. Localized blizzard conditions occurred within this band over northern Herkimer County. The main snow band moved north of the area during the afternoon of the 28th, but returned during the evening into the overnight hours, with a few more additional inches of snow. Total accumulation from the 26th through the 29th ranged from a couple of inches over southern Hamilton County to a foot and a half over northern Herkimer and northern Hamilton Counties.

A couple of rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted eastern New York during the afternoon and evening of the 29th. The first was a couple of isolated cells that produced sporadic wind damage from portions of the Capital District into the Glens Falls area. The second was a broken line of storms that produced a more widespread swath of wind damage in the greater Saratoga-Glens Falls area. An EF1 tornado formed within this line, impacting the area from Lake Luzerne to Glens Falls. There were also areas of significant tree damage in Washington County. Low-hanging wires resulted in the closure of Interstate 87 between exits 17 and 19. More than 8,000 customers lost power in Warren and northern Saratoga Counties due to the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Gabriel Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, May 15, 2020, an intense line of thunderstorms quickly developed near Lake Ontario and raced eastward into eastern New York. A segment of particularly strong and long-lasting thunderstorms impacted the region from just north of the Mohawk Valley in Herkimer County into Saratoga and Washington counties, producing damaging winds throughout its path. Notable areas of damage include a confirmed macroburst near Russia and Cold Brook, NY where wind estimates ranged from 80 to 100 mph, an EF-1 tornado in Wilton, NY, and numerous trees snapped or uprooted in Saratoga and Washington counties. A supercell that developed in the eastern Catskills produced a microburst in Cottekill, NY. Around 71,000 customers were without power at the height of the storm, and the American Red Cross provided emergency aid to 10 people after their homes were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on State Route 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold pattern persisted from April into the growing season across most of the northeastern United States. This allowed for widespread accumulating snows periodically along with unseasonable cold temperatures to persist through the first half of May. Widespread freezing temperatures were present overnight in much of the area on several nights as the first few weeks of the growing season started. Selected morning low temperatures included 27 in Wellsville, 29 in Jamestown, 27 in Avon, 29 in Rochester, 30 in Niagara Falls, 31 in Geneva, and 27 in Wyoming.

Notable areas of damage include a confirmed macroburst near Russia and Cold Brook, NY where wind estimates ranged from 80 to 100 mph, an EF-1 tornado in Wilton, NY, and numerous trees snapped or uprooted in Saratoga and Washington counties. A supercell that developed in the eastern Catskills produced a microburst in Cottekill, NY. Around 71,000 customers were without power at the height of the storm, and the American Red Cross provided emergency aid to 10 people after their homes were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Dunbar Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold pattern persisted from April into the growing season across most of the northeastern United States. This allowed for widespread accumulating snows periodically along with unseasonable cold temperatures to persist through the first half of May. Widespread freezing temperatures were present overnight in much of the area on several nights as the first few weeks of the growing season started. Selected morning low temperatures on May 10 included 29 in Jamestown, 27 in Avon, 31 in Brockport, 30 in Canandaigua, 30 in North Tonawanda, 30 in Sodus, 30 in Webster, and 25 in Warsaw.

A couple of rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted eastern New York during the afternoon and evening of the 29th. The first was a couple of isolated cells that produced sporadic wind damage from portions of the Capital District into the Glens Falls area. The second was a broken line of storms that produced a more widespread swath of wind damage in the greater Saratoga-Glens Falls area. An EF1 tornado formed within this line, impacting the area from Lake Luzerne to Glens Falls. There were also areas of significant tree damage in Washington County. Low-hanging wires resulted in the closure of Interstate 87 between exits 17 and 19. More than 8,000 customers lost power in Warren and northern Saratoga Counties due to the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed, blocking the roadway at Vaughn and Underwood Roads.

Between 1.5 and 2.0 inches of rain fell in the drainage basin of the Walnut Creek upstream from Silver Creek. Because of the geometry of the basin, this is enough to cause a rapid rise of water downstream in the town of Silver Creek. The river gauge installed there indicated a rapid rise to a crest of 7.63 feet, above the nominal flood stage of 7 feet after 5:55 PM. It quickly receded. The areas that include Rix, Oliver, and Dana Streets all flooded and were temporarily evacuated.

The first was a couple of isolated cells that produced sporadic wind damage from portions of the Capital District into the Glens Falls area. The second was a broken line of storms that produced a more widespread swath of wind damage in the greater Saratoga-Glens Falls area. An EF1 tornado formed within this line, impacting the area from Lake Luzerne to Glens Falls. There were also areas of significant tree damage in Washington County. Low-hanging wires resulted in the closure of Interstate 87 between exits 17 and 19. More than 8,000 customers lost power in Warren and northern Saratoga Counties due to the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Route 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, May 15, 2020, an intense line of thunderstorms quickly developed near Lake Ontario and raced eastward into eastern New York. A segment of particularly strong and long-lasting thunderstorms impacted the region from just north of the Mohawk Valley in Herkimer County into Saratoga and Washington counties, producing damaging winds throughout its path. Notable areas of damage include a confirmed macroburst near Russia and Cold Brook, NY where wind estimates ranged from 80 to 100 mph, an EF-1 tornado in Wilton, NY, and numerous trees snapped or uprooted in Saratoga and Washington counties. A supercell that developed in the eastern Catskills produced a microburst in Cottekill, NY. Around 71,000 customers were without power at the height of the storm, and the American Red Cross provided emergency aid to 10 people after their homes were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Riverview Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of tropical storm Bertha advanced across the region combined with very high shear and low instability caused by the passed of a trough to create a couple rounds of heavy showers and thunderstorms. Because of the fast storm movement, widespread flooding did not occur, however storm damage was noted over portions of the area with attendant high winds over marine areas as the storms moved through as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on Woodpecker Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough moved across the area with ample instability ahead of it. Showers and thunderstorms initially formed along the Lake Erie lake breeze then pushed eastward with the cold front. These storms first pulsed in Erie County then formed a bowing segment as they moved eastward, eventually causing a microburst near Wolcott in Wayne County.

These storms first pulsed in Erie County then formed a bowing segment as they moved eastward, eventually causing a microburst near Wolcott in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted eastern New York during the afternoon and evening of the 29th. The first was a couple of isolated cells that produced sporadic wind damage from portions of the Capital District into the Glens Falls area. The second was a broken line of storms that produced a more widespread swath of wind damage in the greater Saratoga-Glens Falls area. An EF1 tornado formed within this line, impacting the area from Lake Luzerne to Glens Falls. There were also areas of significant tree damage in Washington County. Low-hanging wires resulted in the closure of Interstate 87 between exits 17 and 19. More than 8,000 customers lost power in Warren and northern Saratoga Counties due to the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed, blocking County Route 61 in Jackson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, May 15, 2020, an intense line of thunderstorms quickly developed near Lake Ontario and raced eastward into eastern New York. A segment of particularly strong and long-lasting thunderstorms impacted the region from just north of the Mohawk Valley in Herkimer County into Saratoga and Washington counties, producing damaging winds throughout its path. Notable areas of damage include a confirmed macroburst near Russia and Cold Brook, NY where wind estimates ranged from 80 to 100 mph, an EF-1 tornado in Wilton, NY, and numerous trees snapped or uprooted in Saratoga and Washington counties. A supercell that developed in the eastern Catskills produced a microburst in Cottekill, NY. Around 71,000 customers were without power at the height of the storm, and the American Red Cross provided emergency aid to 10 people after their homes were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on State Route 29.

A couple of rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted eastern New York during the afternoon and evening of the 29th. The first was a couple of isolated cells that produced sporadic wind damage from portions of the Capital District into the Glens Falls area. The second was a broken line of storms that produced a more widespread swath of wind damage in the greater Saratoga-Glens Falls area. An EF1 tornado formed within this line, impacting the area from Lake Luzerne to Glens Falls. There were also areas of significant tree damage in Washington County. Low-hanging wires resulted in the closure of Interstate 87 between exits 17 and 19. More than 8,000 customers lost power in Warren and northern Saratoga Counties due to the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the Hudson River close to Hawk Road south of Call Street in Lake Luzerne. It moved in a general northeast direction crossing Hawk Road, Call Street and both ends of Hartman Loop, before continuing along Corinth Road. The tornado continued northeast, crossing Interstate 87 at Exit 18 where it uprooted |trees in the nearby cemetery. The tornado ended in Glens Falls near the Queensbury Hotel. Damage was most extensive west of Interstate 87 along Hawk Road, Call Street, Corinth Road and Hartman Loop eastward to the subdivision on Hudson Point Boulevard where numerous hardwood and softwood trees were sheared off and uprooted along the path. One home on Hartman Loop had damage to its roof. East of Interstate 87, damage was generally less severe, with mainly downed tree limbs and branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-season storm brought accumulating snowfall to portions of Washington County and the Taconics from the evening of the 8th into the early morning of the 9th. Amounts ranged from 3 to 9 inches across Washington County, locally up to a foot in the higher elevations of the Taconics. Around 1000 customers lost power due to the heavy snow. In addition, wind chills fell into the teens and 20s during the morning of the 9th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado hit the Wilton, New York area. The tornado touched down near the intersection of Nichols Road and Woodard Road and tracked east for around two miles before lifting at the intersection of Wilton-Gansevoort Road and Blanchard Road. The path was nearly continuous with several softwood trees snapped and uprooted (some|onto houses), and street signs blown down and displaced. Also, part of the sheeting of a warehouse roof was peeled off and landed on a van, and an empty trailer was blown on top of a van.

A very cold pattern persisted from April into the growing season across most of the northeastern United States. This allowed for widespread accumulating snows periodically along with unseasonable cold temperatures to persist through the first half of May. Widespread freezing temperatures were present overnight in much of the area on several nights as the first few weeks of the growing season started. Selected morning low temperatures on May 8 included 24 in Angelica, 29 in Olean, 30 in Dunkirk, 29 in Avon, 31 in Rochester, 30 in Victor, and 29 in Warsaw.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team, along with Emergency Management, determined that a macroburst impacted the areas of Russia, Norway and Ohio in central Herkimer County. This macroburst resulted in numerous large hardwood trees uprooted or snapped about a third to halfway up along with power poles snapped with significant power outages. Areas hardest hit were along Grant Road and Fisher Road. There were also several trees downed at Pardeeville Road and Dan Davis Road in Ohio. A large tree went through a small house on Fisher Road. A secondary area of damage was in the Adirondack Campground where an un-anchored trailer flipped and was dragged several feet to its resting place. Numerous hardwood trees were also observed to be uprooted or snapped about a third to halfway up. All debris was pointed in an east-northeast direction.

Widespread freezing temperatures were present overnight in much of the area on several nights as the first few weeks of the growing season started. Selected morning low temperatures on May 12 included 29 in Alfred, 29 in Dunkirk, 31 in Buffalo, 32 in Theresa, 30 in Highmarket, 27 in Avon, 30 in Rochester, 24 in Niagara Falls, 30 in Victor, 30 in Webster, 32 in Little Valley, and 26 in Warsaw.

On Friday, May 15, 2020, an intense line of thunderstorms quickly developed near Lake Ontario and raced eastward into eastern New York. A segment of particularly strong and long-lasting thunderstorms impacted the region from just north of the Mohawk Valley in Herkimer County into Saratoga and Washington counties, producing damaging winds throughout its path. Notable areas of damage include a confirmed macroburst near Russia and Cold Brook, NY where wind estimates ranged from 80 to 100 mph, an EF-1 tornado in Wilton, NY, and numerous trees snapped or uprooted in Saratoga and Washington counties. A supercell that developed in the eastern Catskills produced a microburst in Cottekill, NY. Around 71,000 customers were without power at the height of the storm, and the American Red Cross provided emergency aid to 10 people after their homes were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees blocked all lanes of Route 9.

The remnants of tropical storm Bertha advanced across the region combined with very high shear and low instability caused by the passed of a trough to create a couple rounds of heavy showers and thunderstorms. Because of the fast storm movement, widespread flooding did not occur, however storm damage was noted over portions of the area with attendant high winds over marine areas as the storms moved through as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down onto Hoffman Road.

A couple of rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted eastern New York during the afternoon and evening of the 29th. The first was a couple of isolated cells that produced sporadic wind damage from portions of the Capital District into the Glens Falls area. The second was a broken line of storms that produced a more widespread swath of wind damage in the greater Saratoga-Glens Falls area. An EF1 tornado formed within this line, impacting the area from Lake Luzerne to Glens Falls. There were also areas of significant tree damage in Washington County. Low-hanging wires resulted in the closure of Interstate 87 between exits 17 and 19. More than 8,000 customers lost power in Warren and northern Saratoga Counties due to the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed onto wires.

A segment of particularly strong and long-lasting thunderstorms impacted the region from just north of the Mohawk Valley in Herkimer County into Saratoga and Washington counties, producing damaging winds throughout its path. Notable areas of damage include a confirmed macroburst near Russia and Cold Brook, NY where wind estimates ranged from 80 to 100 mph, an EF-1 tornado in Wilton, NY, and numerous trees snapped or uprooted in Saratoga and Washington counties. A supercell that developed in the eastern Catskills produced a microburst in Cottekill, NY. Around 71,000 customers were without power at the height of the storm, and the American Red Cross provided emergency aid to 10 people after their homes were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Hegeman Bridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold pattern persisted from April into the growing season across most of the northeastern United States. This allowed for widespread accumulating snows periodically along with unseasonable cold temperatures to persist through the first half of May. Widespread freezing temperatures were present overnight in much of the area on several nights as the first few weeks of the growing season started. Selected morning low temperatures on May 13 included 21 in Angelica, 25 in Olean, 27 in Dunkirk, 30 in Buffalo, 27 in Watertown, 22 in Highmarket, 25 in Avon, 25 in North Tonawanda, 24 in Honeoye, 24 in Lakeside, 29 in Oswego, and 26 in Warsaw.

A low pressure system brought a mix of rain and snow to the region Sunday, April 26th into Monday, April 27th, 2020. Temperatures cooled aloft, resulting in rain changing over to snow across high elevations. Snow totals ranged from 3 inches up to 8 inches across portions of the eastern Catskills, Schoharie Valley, Mohawk Valley and the southern Adirondacks. Some power outages also occurred as a result of the snowfall, mainly across the Mohawk Valley into the southern Adirondacks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and a warm front, along with a convectively-enhanced shortwave, drove a complex of thunderstorms across the lower Great Lakes late in the evening. Though the bulk of the complex remained over Ohio into Pennsylvania, a small cluster dropped across mainly western and southern Chautauqua county. Though the storms initially appeared elevated, strong low-level shear allowed for a burst of strong winds to make it to the surface near Sherman. The same cell later pulsed up near Jamestown and produced severe hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure passed to the west of the area resulting in high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Islip MacArthur Airport measured a 61 mph wind gust at 147 pm. At 158 pm, sustained winds of 45 mph were measured at Islip MacArthur Airport. Law enforcement reported wires down on Straight Path Road blocking Old Country Road at 1156 am. At 1209 pm, the fire department reported wires down on Mobile Street in Sayville. Around 1230 pm in the town of Brentwood, law enforcement reported a tree down blocking Eisenhower Avenue at Saint Andrews. A mesonet station near the Great South Bay measured 45 mph sustained winds at 133 pm. Around 138 pm, another mesonet station located at the Fire Island Coast Guard station measured 44 mph sustained winds. At 146 pm, a mesonet station near the town of Brookhaven measured a 60 mph wind gust. At that same time, sustained winds were 46 mph. Around 210 pm, law enforcement reported a tree with wires down on River Road in Shirley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system impacted the region throughout the day on Monday, April 13th, 2020. This resulted in severe thunderstorms and strong to damaging winds across the eastern New York, with several reports of trees and power lines downed. Some of the hardest hit areas were in the Taconics and in Dutchess county where 20,000 people lost power due to the high winds.

A pre-frontal trough moved into seasonable warm, humid and unstable air during the afternoon of May 29th. Multiple rounds of thunderstorms, some multi cell clusters and some discreet moved across NY into VT.  These thunderstorms contained strong to damaging winds and brief torrential rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down along Route 86 north of?Lake Placid.

Heavy rainfall from an approaching weather system persisted for several hours. A locally enhanced line of thunderstorm activity triggered rapid and enhanced flooding in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rapidly rushed through Moravia, NY along Mill Creek and into the Owasco Lake Inlet. A dozen structures in the heart of the village experienced minor to moderate flood damage. One structure was heavily damaged by flooding. The Owasco Inlet crested at 9.44 feet which was near record flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought a mix of rain and snow to the region Sunday, April 26th into Monday, April 27th, 2020. Temperatures cooled aloft, resulting in rain changing over to snow across high elevations. Snow totals ranged from 3 inches up to 8 inches across portions of the eastern Catskills, Schoharie Valley, Mohawk Valley and the southern Adirondacks. Some power outages also occurred as a result of the snowfall, mainly across the Mohawk Valley into the southern Adirondacks.

Post-frontal winds mixed well behind an early morning cold front. This brought wind gusts across much of western New York, especially along the Lake Erie shore, Buffalo, and Batavia area that exceeded 65 mph. Widespread non-thunderstorm wind damage was reported in all lakeshore counties from Monroe westward along Lake Ontario and all counties bordering Lake Erie, as well. High winds drove a seiche on Lake Erie, resulting in water flooding Route 5 in Hamburg, additional damage to the Dunkirk Pier and break wall, damage to the Buffalo break wall, and flooding in Canalside in downtown Buffalo. The seiche peaked the water level in Buffalo at 9.85 feet above low water datum.

A relatively deep, progressive mid-level trough crossed southern Ontario and the Lower Great Lakes Saturday night and early Sunday, January 18-19.  The associated weak surface low tracked across southern Ontario in the process with widespread mixed synoptic precipitation eventually giving way to disorganized lake snows by daybreak Sunday. As the cold air deepened and the cap rose to nearly 10,000 feet, multiple bands of moderate to occasionally heavy lake snow became established east of the lakes. Selected snow totals included 19 in Osceola, 12 in Redfield, 21.5 in Java Village, 23 in Wyoming, 20.9 in Perrysburg, 8.9 in Watertown, and 13 in Forestville. As the cold front passed that brought colder air in, a seiche on Lake Erie again brought lakeshore flooding to Chautauqua and Erie counties, doing further damage to the Dunkirk Pier, Buffalo break wall, and closing Route 5 in Hamburg. There were two distinct pulses of higher water on the east end of the lake, as well. The first corresponded with lake levels of 8.18 feet above low water datum, and the second of 7.3 feet above low water datum.

After an unseasonably warm spell, strong westerly winds developed behind a cold frontal passage during the morning and afternoon of January 12th. The winds gusted as high as 54 miles per hour and resulted in a few reports of downed branches and trees. There was also some roof damage to a gas station near Glens Falls.

Strong northwesterly winds developed in the wake of a strengthening low pressure system offshore of Cape Cod during the late morning to early evening of January 16th. Winds gusting as high as 47 miles per hour brought down a few large branches. The winds brought in a frigid airmass, with wind chills in the southern Adirondacks falling as low as 25 below zero during the early morning of the 17th.

Strong, intensifying low pressure moved across Ontario during the afternoon of April 13th. A strong associated cold front moved across northern NY during the afternoon and evening hours bringing locally heavy rainfall and strong winds. West-southwest winds gusted 45-60 mph across much of the St. Lawrence valley producing some localized wind damage in terms of downed trees, branches falling onto utility lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NY State mesonet sight in Malone measured 60 mph wind gust with neighboring sites gusting in the low to mid 50 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred behind a cold front and deepening low pressure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at JFK International Airport measured a 41 mph sustained wind around 240 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, intensifying low pressure moved across Ontario during the afternoon of April 13th. A strong associated cold front moved across northern NY during the afternoon and evening hours bringing locally heavy rainfall and strong winds. West-southwest winds gusted 45-60 mph across much of the St. Lawrence valley producing some localized wind damage in terms of downed trees, branches falling onto utility lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked to the north and west of Central New York with snow quickly overspreading the area during the morning hours of Saturday, January 18th, persisting through the day and evening. Then a lake effect snow band developed and impacted the area during the morning hours of the 19th. The combination of the snow from the synoptic system and lake effect behind it allowed for heavy snow accumulations across northern Oneida County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 5 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cyclonic flow across the Great Lakes allowed cold air to filter into western and north central New York through the start of the new year. A surface low tracked northeast from northern Michigan to northeastern Ontario Monday night into Tuesday morning. An associated cold front tracked across western and north central New York Tuesday resulting in widespread rain and snow showers across the region. The cold, westerly flow behind the front transitioned widespread showers to lake effect snow showers east of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. Lake temperatures averaged in the upper 30s to low 40s during this event with no ice present, which allowed some warmer surface air to make its way into the lower terrain east of the lakes, limiting accumulation there. In the higher terrain, however, heavy accumulations were very isolated to 12.3 at Perrysburg east of Lake Erie and more widespread east of Lake Ontario with locally heavy accumulations of 13.0 in Rector and 10.4 in Redfield.

A weak mid-level trough moved from northern Ontario into western Quebec on Monday|January 6. A weak surface low took a similar track, reaching James Bay by late in|the afternoon. A trailing cold front moved east across Lake Ontario Monday morning, |bringing with it an air mass that was marginally cold enough to support lake effect |snow. The mid-level trough continued to move east across Quebec Monday night, with |the marginally supportive setup for lake effect snow east of Lake Ontario quickly ending by late evening as the associated pool of cold air aloft and deeper moisture moved east of the area. Selected event total accumulations include 9.4 in Redfield, 12 in Martinsburg, and 11 in Constableville.

Post-frontal winds mixed well behind an early morning cold front. This brought wind gusts across much of western New York, especially along the Lake Erie shore, Buffalo, and Batavia area that exceeded 65 mph. Widespread non-thunderstorm wind damage was reported in all lakeshore counties from Monroe westward along Lake Ontario and all counties bordering Lake Erie, as well. High winds drove a seiche on Lake Erie, resulting in water flooding Route 5 in Hamburg, additional damage to the Dunkirk Pier and break wall, damage to the Buffalo break wall, and flooding in Canalside in downtown Buffalo. The seiche peaked the water level in Buffalo at 9.85 feet above low water datum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure strengthened rapidly as it tracked from the mid-Mississippi River Valley across the central Great Lakes down to 972 hPa. In addition to a favorable track for high winds, 925 hPa winds increased to around 70 knots and mixed behind the front. This lead to high wind gusts and a seiche developing on Lake Erie that peaked at 9.11 feet above low water datum. The maximum wind gusts reported across the area were 65 mph at the Fredonia mesonet station and 61 mph at the Brant mesonet station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in multiple portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow occurred in the western Adirondacks ahead of a strengthening low pressure system from the late evening of the 26th into the morning of the 27th. As the low continued to strengthen north of the area, cold air poured in and windy conditions developed. This allowed a lake effect snow band to form and persist from the morning of the 27th into the afternoon of the 28th. Localized blizzard conditions occurred within this band over northern Herkimer County. The main snow band moved north of the area during the afternoon of the 28th, but returned during the evening into the overnight hours, with a few more additional inches of snow. Total accumulation from the 26th through the 29th ranged from a couple of inches over southern Hamilton County to a foot and a half over northern Herkimer and northern Hamilton Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of wintry precipitation impacted portions of eastern New York on the 6th and 7th. The first was a round of light to moderate snow after midnight on 6th to around sunrise, which resulted in one to five inches of snow along and north of the I-88/I-90 corridor. Then, periods of freezing rain occurred from the afternoon of the 6th into the morning of the 7th mainly along and north of I-90. Reports of a quarter to a half an inch of ice accretion were received in the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks into Washington County. The freezing rain quickly turned to heavy snow during the afternoon of the 7th as a strong low pressure system passed south of the area. This round of snow resulted in a quick 6-12 inches of accumulation in the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Lake George Saratoga Region, with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour were common. In the wake of the snowfall, westerly winds gusted to 40-55 miles per hour in the afternoon and evening of the 7th, highest south of I-90.||The weight of the heavy snow and ice on trees along with strong winds resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines. Nearly 90,000 customers lost power, with Saratoga (46,000) and Warren (22,000) Counties being hit the hardest. Over half of Warren County was without power, prompting warming shelters to be opened. Several accidents occurred due to the snowy and icy roads across the southern Adirondacks and Lake George Saratoga Region, including one fatality in Mayfield, and major damage to a fire engine in Chester. A man in Lake Luzerne was injured when he was pierced in the leg while driving a vehicle, and a Thurman Highway Department truck was damaged when a tree fell onto it. Travel was discouraged due to the conditions in Saratoga Springs.

A cold front just south of the region gradually retreated back to the north during the morning hours. This boundary served as a focus for the development of showers and thunderstorms. With weak flow aloft and a moist environment, this resulted in flash flooding across portions of northern Nassau County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hill Road was closed due to flooding in Port Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms developed across Central New York during the afternoon of the 13th ahead of an approaching strong cold front. Some of these storms became severe and caused mainly tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds brought down a tree on top of powerlines on Wheaton Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds brought down powerlines at Hilton Road and Route 434.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms developed across Central New York during the afternoon of the 13th ahead of an approaching strong cold front. Some of these storms became severe and caused mainly tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds brought down a tree across a roadway near Watson Blvd. and Hayes Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure strengthened rapidly as it tracked from the mid-Mississippi River Valley across the central Great Lakes down to 972 hPa. In addition to a favorable track for high winds, 925 hPa winds increased to around 70 knots and mixed behind the front. This lead to high wind gusts and a seiche developing on Lake Erie that peaked at 9.11 feet above low water datum. The maximum wind gusts reported across the area were 65 mph at the Fredonia mesonet station and 61 mph at the Brant mesonet station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water level on Lake Erie peaked at 9.11 feet above low water datum at 4:48 PM EDT. This caused additional damage in Dunkirk.

Several accidents occurred due to the snowy and icy roads across the southern Adirondacks and Lake George Saratoga Region, including one fatality in Mayfield, and major damage to a fire engine in Chester. A man in Lake Luzerne was injured when he was pierced in the leg while driving a vehicle, and a Thurman Highway Department truck was damaged when a tree fell onto it. Travel was discouraged due to the conditions in Saratoga Springs.

A cold front straddled across NY and VT during the February 6-7th time frame. A weak system, moved across the North country during the early morning hours of February 6th with snowfall of 1 to 3 inches along the International border to 2 to 4 inches across the southern Adirondacks. During the day and evening hours of February 6th, intermittent snow and freezing rain showers still allowed for some slick travel ahead of the more vigorous system.||During the late evening and overnight hours of the 6th-7th, steadier snowfall arrived across the St. Lawrence River Valley eventually moving into the rest of northern NY early on the 7th. Precipitation in the southern Adirondacks and Champlain Valley was freezing rain and sleet early before changing to heavy snowfall by mid-morning with snowfall rates of 1 to 2+ inches per hour through early afternoon. Snowfall for Friday across northern NY was generally 8 to 18 inches.||Two-day snowfall across the area was generally 10 to 20 inches with icing under 1/4 inch across the southern Champlain Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall of 12 to 20 inches fell across the region with more than two-thirds of that occurring with the second phase on February 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure advanced from the Ohio Valley toward central New York. Model guidance waffled all over with the track of this system in the days before its arrival, eventually placing the low on a more southerly track. This resulted in widespread snow across the area rather than a mix, and it also yielded an extended period of west-northwesterly flow lake effect snow in its wake. Because of a lack of cold air outbreaks during the course of the winter, both Lake Erie and Ontario were nearly ice free, resulting in heavy snow squalls with very gusty conditions across the Southern Tier and Tug Hill regions. Widespread accumulations of 2-3 feet occurred east of Lake Ontario with 1-2 feet common east of Lake Erie. Selected specific snow accumulations include 8.9 inches in Lockport, 31.2 inches in Redfield, 48 inches in Carthage, 28.5 inches in Copenhagen, 14.5 inches in Warsaw, 12.5 inches in Jamestown, 20.3 inches in Perrysburg, and 25 inches in Springville. Selected wind gusts included measured gusts of 62 mph at Fredonia, 60 mph at Oswego, 60 mph at Watertown, and 56 mph at Hamburg.||In addition to snow and wind, lakeshore flooding with the passage of the system became a problem on Lake Erie. The biggest seiche of the month occurred during the event, bringing the lake level up to nearly 7.5 feet above low water datum at Buffalo on the 27th.

After a day when temperatures soared into the low to mid 90s across the bulk of western New York, severe convection broke out. A solid line of strong to severe thunderstorms crossed Ohio and western Lake Erie ahead of a cold front late in the afternoon. Meanwhile, our region remain capped with very unstable air aloft but weak shear. As the convection to our west approached during the evening, a distinct outflow boundary emerged and raced across the Niagara Peninsula. This outflow is where the majority of the wind damage came from. The outflow made it all the way to Monroe County, where additional damage was reported. Additional thunderstorms developed over the Southern Tier and produced damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto a trailer.

A storm system developed along a frontal boundary located through the Mid-Atlantic region during the late afternoon and evening of the 6th. This storm rapidly intensified throughout the morning hours of the 7th, leading to a quick period of heavy snowfall across Central New York during the morning and early afternoon hours. Lingering lake effect snow showers then developed southeast of Lake Ontario as the storm pulled away during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 8 to 12 inches.

Scattered pulse thunderstorms developed quickly midday well ahead of an approaching weak shortwave lifting in from northeast Ohio. The environment was unstable while shear was weak due to subdued wind speeds aloft. A few of the storms showed very strong lightning jumps on satellite imagery. Heavy rain was also a concern with rates that exceeded one to two inches per hour in strongest storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down near route 280 and I-86 at the Allegany State Park entrance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure advanced from the Ohio Valley toward central New York. Model guidance waffled all over with the track of this system in the days before its arrival, eventually placing the low on a more southerly track. This resulted in widespread snow across the area rather than a mix, and it also yielded an extended period of west-northwesterly flow lake effect snow in its wake. Because of a lack of cold air outbreaks during the course of the winter, both Lake Erie and Ontario were nearly ice free, resulting in heavy snow squalls with very gusty conditions across the Southern Tier and Tug Hill regions. Widespread accumulations of 2-3 feet occurred east of Lake Ontario with 1-2 feet common east of Lake Erie. Selected specific snow accumulations include 8.9 inches in Lockport, 31.2 inches in Redfield, 48 inches in Carthage, 28.5 inches in Copenhagen, 14.5 inches in Warsaw, 12.5 inches in Jamestown, 20.3 inches in Perrysburg, and 25 inches in Springville. Selected wind gusts included measured gusts of 62 mph at Fredonia, 60 mph at Oswego, 60 mph at Watertown, and 56 mph at Hamburg.||In addition to snow and wind, lakeshore flooding with the passage of the system became a problem on Lake Erie. The biggest seiche of the month occurred during the event, bringing the lake level up to nearly 7.5 feet above low water datum at Buffalo on the 27th.

A lake effect snow band developed during the evening hours of the 19th, drifting south across Onondaga county overnight. The heaviest snow totals were found along and north of the NY Thruway, especially in Onondaga County. The snow tapered off and ended by late morning on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 6 and 8 inches in the northern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system developed along a frontal boundary located through the Mid-Atlantic region during the late afternoon and evening of the 6th. This storm rapidly intensified throughout the morning hours of the 7th, leading to a quick period of heavy snowfall across Central New York during the morning and early afternoon hours. Lingering lake effect snow showers then developed southeast of Lake Ontario as the storm pulled away during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elongated storm system intensified across New England-NY on February 27th and then slowed forward progression across Quebec on February 28th. A wintry mix of snow and rain fell on the 26th and morning hours of the 27th before changing to snow/snow showers at midday. A prolonged west-southwest flow of cold, unstable air across the long fetch of Lake Ontario, developed a very pronounced and intense lake effect snow band south of the region during the afternoon of February 27th, that eventually rotated north into the region by the evening and eventually left the area by midday of February 28th.||A narrow band of 12 to 20 inches of snow fell across southeast St. Lawrence county in the vicinity of Route 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of 12 to 20 inches of snow fell across the region along the Route 3 corridor, including; 22 inches in Star Lake, 20 inches in Wanakena and 12 inches in Cranberry Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warmer air aloft resided over the area before the arrival of a system that bombed out as it moved into the Middle Atlantic states. Widespread freezing drizzle and freezing rain resided over the area until deeper lift from the deformation zone of this aforementioned system allowed for dynamic cooling to affect the column overhead from the Finger Lakes toward the areas east of Lake Ontario. This allowed for a swath of heavy snow across the region, however upslope and lake enhanced snows in its wake weren't as impressive and largely over-forecast. Selected snow totals for the event include 13 inches in Redfield, 10.4 inches in Watertown, and 14.7 inches in Lowville. Amounts near or over one foot were common east of Lake Ontario.

A weak system moved across New York, spreading light snow that quickly changed to rain over far western New York. This snow greatly affected the morning commute in Rochester on 2/18, but amounts were not significant. Cold air in its wake moved back into the region on 2/19 with strong northwesterly flow. This resulted in lake effect snow accumulations approaching a foot over much of the southeast shore of Lake Ontario. Selected totals included 11 inches in Phoenix, 12 in Wolcott, and 10 inches in Victory.

A long wave ridge with a weak upper low to our south resulted in a very weak flow aloft. The Buffalo sounding indicated only a moderately moist column with 1.25 inches of precipitable water. As had happened the past couple days, diurnal instability resulted in late afternoon and early evening thunderstorms. One storm stalled near Mount Morris and Letchworth State Park, back-building briefly, before weakening. Radar estimates eventually reached 3.7 inches in this storm, with the emergency manager reporting up to 5 inches of rain in a small core of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 408, Ridge Road, Creed Road, River Road, Dutch Road, and Cleveland Road were all closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered pulse thunderstorms developed quickly midday well ahead of an approaching weak shortwave lifting in from northeast Ohio. The environment was unstable while shear was weak due to subdued wind speeds aloft. A few of the storms showed very strong lightning jumps on satellite imagery. Heavy rain was also a concern with rates that exceeded one to two inches per hour in strongest storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front straddled across NY and VT during the February 6-7th time frame. A weak system, moved across the North country during the early morning hours of February 6th with snowfall of 1 to 3 inches along the International border to 2 to 4 inches across the southern Adirondacks. During the day and evening hours of February 6th, intermittent snow and freezing rain showers still allowed for some slick travel ahead of the more vigorous system.||During the late evening and overnight hours of the 6th-7th, steadier snowfall arrived across the St. Lawrence River Valley eventually moving into the rest of northern NY early on the 7th. Precipitation in the southern Adirondacks and Champlain Valley was freezing rain and sleet early before changing to heavy snowfall by mid-morning with snowfall rates of 1 to 2+ inches per hour through early afternoon. Snowfall for Friday across northern NY was generally 8 to 18 inches.||Two-day snowfall across the area was generally 10 to 20 inches with icing under 1/4 inch across the southern Champlain Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall of 12 to 18 inches fell across the region with more than two-thirds of that occurring with the second phase on February 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of a prolonged heat wave was upon the area with modestly unstable air ahead of a cold front slicing through the Upper Great Lakes. The few storms that developed exhibited little structure on radar, however one cell just north of the Buffalo radar managed to grow to above 30 kft. This cell had a lifespan of about 25 minutes, but in that time put down a downburst that resulted in damage in the North Tonawanda and Wheatfield areas. A second cell with similar lifespan put down a similar wind gust nearby.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of trees and wires down were received from North Tonawanda and Wheatfield.

A precipitable water value of 1.65 inches was observed on the KBUF sounding, and models suggested over 2 inches in portions of the area. This combined with an incoming mesoscale convective vortex to drive slow-moving and heavy rain-producing thunderstorms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.72 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of wintry precipitation impacted portions of eastern New York on the 6th and 7th. The first was a round of light to moderate snow after midnight on 6th to around sunrise, which resulted in one to five inches of snow along and north of the I-88/I-90 corridor. Then, periods of freezing rain occurred from the afternoon of the 6th into the morning of the 7th mainly along and north of I-90. Reports of a quarter to a half an inch of ice accretion were received in the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks into Washington County. The freezing rain quickly turned to heavy snow during the afternoon of the 7th as a strong low pressure system passed south of the area. This round of snow resulted in a quick 6-12 inches of accumulation in the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Lake George Saratoga Region, with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour were common. In the wake of the snowfall, westerly winds gusted to 40-55 miles per hour in the afternoon and evening of the 7th, highest south of I-90.||The weight of the heavy snow and ice on trees along with strong winds resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines. Nearly 90,000 customers lost power, with Saratoga (46,000) and Warren (22,000) Counties being hit the hardest. Over half of Warren County was without power, prompting warming shelters to be opened. Several accidents occurred due to the snowy and icy roads across the southern Adirondacks and Lake George Saratoga Region, including one fatality in Mayfield, and major damage to a fire engine in Chester. A man in Lake Luzerne was injured when he was pierced in the leg while driving a vehicle, and a Thurman Highway Department truck was damaged when a tree fell onto it. Travel was discouraged due to the conditions in Saratoga Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and very moist air mass was in place across the region with dewpoints in the 70s and precipitable water values approaching 2 inches. Diurnal instability along a lake breeze boundary allowed for convection to develop. The most notable thing about these boundaries was the intersection of the boundary off of Lake Erie with a weaker one south of Lake Ontario. Thunderstorms developed and trained along this intersection for hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.03 inches fell in less than 90 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of wintry precipitation impacted portions of eastern New York on the 6th and 7th. The first was a round of light to moderate snow after midnight on 6th to around sunrise, which resulted in one to five inches of snow along and north of the I-88/I-90 corridor. Then, periods of freezing rain occurred from the afternoon of the 6th into the morning of the 7th mainly along and north of I-90. Reports of a quarter to a half an inch of ice accretion were received in the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks into Washington County. The freezing rain quickly turned to heavy snow during the afternoon of the 7th as a strong low pressure system passed south of the area. This round of snow resulted in a quick 6-12 inches of accumulation in the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Lake George Saratoga Region, with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour were common. In the wake of the snowfall, westerly winds gusted to 40-55 miles per hour in the afternoon and evening of the 7th, highest south of I-90.||The weight of the heavy snow and ice on trees along with strong winds resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines. Nearly 90,000 customers lost power, with Saratoga (46,000) and Warren (22,000) Counties being hit the hardest. Over half of Warren County was without power, prompting warming shelters to be opened. Several accidents occurred due to the snowy and icy roads across the southern Adirondacks and Lake George Saratoga Region, including one fatality in Mayfield, and major damage to a fire engine in Chester. A man in Lake Luzerne was injured when he was pierced in the leg while driving a vehicle, and a Thurman Highway Department truck was damaged when a tree fell onto it. Travel was discouraged due to the conditions in Saratoga Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex pulled a pair of fronts across the area during the afternoon and evening near the time of peak heating. Due to cloud cover ahead of the MCV, instability was not very strong, however shear was impressive. Helicity values were impressive, and the presence of the MCV likely added to it locally over the western Southern Tier. Precipitable water values were between 1.75 and 2 inches, which resulted in torrential rainfall in concert with severe thunderstorm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 242 at Dutch Hill Road closed due to flooding.

Excessive heat returned to the St. Lawrence Valley on what has already been a hot and dry early summer. High temperatures surpassed 90 degrees from July 7-10th with a record daily high of 99 degrees on July 10th. This was the 2nd highest temperature ALL-TIME at Massena, NY. In addition, high dewpoints in the upper 60s to lower 70s created heat index values at or above 100 degrees in the St. Lawrence Valley on the 9th and 10th.||In fact, Massena's max temperature for the first 10 days of July has averaged 90.8 degrees, which broke the old record of 90.3 degrees set in 1973.||Some of this excessive heat migrated into the Champlain Valley on the 8th-10th with highs in the 90s and heat index values in the mid to upper 90s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in the 90s with dewpoints in the 60s to around 70 created heat indices in the mid to upper 90s.

The tornado touched down in a wooded area before moving across a property, flattening a 60'x80' garage and lifting and rotating a 53' semi trailer. It then crossed a road and damaged the second story of a barn. It crossed another road and damaged two other barns  along Thayer Road before continuing to tree damage to the east end of Farr Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable air mass developed as temperatures rose into the lower 90s and surface dewpoints eclipsed 70 degrees. CAPE values increased to over 3000 J/kg while shear was near 50 knots. A mesoscale convective complex over lower Michigan in the morning maintained is strength across Ontario. With strong southwesterly flow up Lake Erie ahead of it, it maintained its strength as it moved east of Lake Erie, while weakening to the north. Considerable wind reports were received on Lake Erie as well as across the Southern Tier and Finger Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down onto Sand Pond Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and very moist air mass was in place across the region with dewpoints in the 70s and precipitable water values approaching 2 inches. Diurnal instability along a lake breeze boundary allowed for convection to develop. The most notable thing about these boundaries was the intersection of the boundary off of Lake Erie with a weaker one south of Lake Ontario. Thunderstorms developed and trained along this intersection for hours. The net result was some of the heaviest rains our area has ever seen in such a short duration with amounts of between 7 and 8 inches reported in Orleans County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ditches were overflowing and running across roads. In 3.5 hours, 5.10 inches of rainfall was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Fay tracked northward along the New Jersey coast before passing west of New York City. Several rounds of heavy rain occurred over the New York City metro area as the storm approached. This rain developed in a tropical airmass, with precipitable water values over 2 inches. Some of the highest rainfall totals occurred in New York City, where the ASOS in Central Park reported 2.43 inches of rain, and New York State Mesonet stations reported 2.96 inches in Midtown Manhattan, 2.44 inches in Brooklyn, 2.21 inches in Staten Island, 2.17 inches in the Bronx, and 2.08 inches in Queens.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Farragut Road was closed due to flooding east of Utica Avenue by Kings Highway in East Flatbush, Brooklyn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A leading surface boundary sagged from lower Michigan across the Southern Tier and Genesee Valley. Ample moisture pooling evidenced by precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 inches and modest shear generated instability that allowed for thunderstorms to vertically develop into a layer of dry air aloft. This provided the first set of wind damage producing thunderstorms. Subsequent to those, the Lake Ontario lake breeze forced additional thunderstorms that keyed on much higher low level helicity that moved southeastward from Genesee to Ontario counties. These storms developed notable rotation, resulting in one tornado in Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires feel onto a house along State Route 394.
