EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of wintry precipitation impacted portions of eastern New York on the 6th and 7th. The first was a round of light to moderate snow after midnight on 6th to around sunrise, which resulted in one to five inches of snow along and north of the I-88/I-90 corridor. Then, periods of freezing rain occurred from the afternoon of the 6th into the morning of the 7th mainly along and north of I-90. Reports of a quarter to a half an inch of ice accretion were received in the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks into Washington County. The freezing rain quickly turned to heavy snow during the afternoon of the 7th as a strong low pressure system passed south of the area. This round of snow resulted in a quick 6-12 inches of accumulation in the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Lake George Saratoga Region, with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour were common. In the wake of the snowfall, westerly winds gusted to 40-55 miles per hour in the afternoon and evening of the 7th, highest south of I-90.||The weight of the heavy snow and ice on trees along with strong winds resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines. Nearly 90,000 customers lost power, with Saratoga (46,000) and Warren (22,000) Counties being hit the hardest. Over half of Warren County was without power, prompting warming shelters to be opened. Several accidents occurred due to the snowy and icy roads across the southern Adirondacks and Lake George Saratoga Region, including one fatality in Mayfield, and major damage to a fire engine in Chester. A man in Lake Luzerne was injured when he was pierced in the leg while driving a vehicle, and a Thurman Highway Department truck was damaged when a tree fell onto it. Travel was discouraged due to the conditions in Saratoga Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat returned to the St. Lawrence Valley on what has already been a hot and dry early summer. High temperatures surpassed 90 degrees from July 7-10th with a record daily high of 99 degrees on July 10th. This was the 2nd highest temperature ALL-TIME at Massena, NY. In addition, high dewpoints in the upper 60s to lower 70s created heat index values at or above 100 degrees in the St. Lawrence Valley on the 9th and 10th.||In fact, Massena's max temperature for the first 10 days of July has averaged 90.8 degrees, which broke the old record of 90.3 degrees set in 1973.||Some of this excessive heat migrated into the Champlain Valley on the 8th-10th with highs in the 90s and heat index values in the mid to upper 90s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in the mid to upper 90s with dew points in the 60s to around 70 created heat indices in the mid 90s to lower 100s. Massena reached 99 degrees on July 10th, 2nd hottest ALL-TIME since records kept in 1948.

Ample moisture pooling evidenced by precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 inches and modest shear generated instability that allowed for thunderstorms to vertically develop into a layer of dry air aloft. This provided the first set of wind damage producing thunderstorms. Subsequent to those, the Lake Ontario lake breeze forced additional thunderstorms that keyed on much higher low level helicity that moved southeastward from Genesee to Ontario counties. These storms developed notable rotation, resulting in one tornado in Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were reported down in Canandaigua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of wintry precipitation impacted portions of eastern New York on the 6th and 7th. The first was a round of light to moderate snow after midnight on 6th to around sunrise, which resulted in one to five inches of snow along and north of the I-88/I-90 corridor. Then, periods of freezing rain occurred from the afternoon of the 6th into the morning of the 7th mainly along and north of I-90. Reports of a quarter to a half an inch of ice accretion were received in the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks into Washington County. The freezing rain quickly turned to heavy snow during the afternoon of the 7th as a strong low pressure system passed south of the area. This round of snow resulted in a quick 6-12 inches of accumulation in the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Lake George Saratoga Region, with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour were common. In the wake of the snowfall, westerly winds gusted to 40-55 miles per hour in the afternoon and evening of the 7th, highest south of I-90.||The weight of the heavy snow and ice on trees along with strong winds resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines. Nearly 90,000 customers lost power, with Saratoga (46,000) and Warren (22,000) Counties being hit the hardest. Over half of Warren County was without power, prompting warming shelters to be opened. Several accidents occurred due to the snowy and icy roads across the southern Adirondacks and Lake George Saratoga Region, including one fatality in Mayfield, and major damage to a fire engine in Chester. A man in Lake Luzerne was injured when he was pierced in the leg while driving a vehicle, and a Thurman Highway Department truck was damaged when a tree fell onto it. Travel was discouraged due to the conditions in Saratoga Springs.

Excessive heat returned to the St. Lawrence Valley on what has already been a hot and dry early summer. High temperatures surpassed 90 degrees from July 7-10th with a record daily high of 99 degrees on July 10th. This was the 2nd highest temperature ALL-TIME at Massena, NY. In addition, high dewpoints in the upper 60s to lower 70s created heat index values at or above 100 degrees in the St. Lawrence Valley on the 9th and 10th.||In fact, Massena's max temperature for the first 10 days of July has averaged 90.8 degrees, which broke the old record of 90.3 degrees set in 1973.||Some of this excessive heat migrated into the Champlain Valley on the 8th-10th with highs in the 90s and heat index values in the mid to upper 90s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in the mid to upper 90s with dew points in the 60s to around 70 created heat indices in the mid 90s to lower 100s. Massena reached 99 degrees on July 10th, 2nd hottest ALL-TIME since records kept in 1948.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of the Christmas Day storm, cold air over the warm waters of Lake Ontario developed a band of lake-effect snow across northern portions of Herkimer County from Saturday, December 26 to Sunday, December 27. The heaviest of the band mainly affected forest areas well to the north of Route 28 and the town of Old Forge. Observations from bordering counties within the band (Lewis and St. Lawrence) support snowfall totals of 10 and 16 inches across northwestern Herkimer County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture conditions, groundwater and surface water flows were considerably below normal for the prior three months over portions of Oneida County. Rainfall deficits were on the order of 2.5 to 3.5 inches below the seasonal normal. The stresses of drought were severe on crops and livestock in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared the northern portion of Oneida county in Severe drought conditions during the month of October due to lack of rainfall and abnormally low surface and groundwater states. Reports of crop and livestock stress were received from county agencies and the U.S. Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture conditions, groundwater and surface water flows were considerably below normal for the prior three months over portions of northern Oneida County. Rainfall deficits were on the order of 2.5 to 3.5 inches below the seasonal normal. The stresses of drought were severe on crops and livestock in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor continued Severe drought conditions over a portion of Oneida county during the month of November, ending on November 17th. A lengthy period with significant precipitation deficits during the past 3 to 6 months was gradually alleviated by rainfall and an improvement in soil moisture and surface water conditions. Harvesting of the final late season crops and loss over natural foliage cover also lessened evapotranspiration helping the rebound toward normal wetness conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system tracked across southern Canada during the evening and night of November 15th. A strong pressure gradient delivered 70-80 knot winds at and above 5000 feet from the surface. A cold front triggered some convective showers with a few isolated updrafts that tapped into the strong winds and briefly delivered 50+ knot winds to the surface in portions of St. Lawrence county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St. Lawrence county Fire control reported several incidents of downed tree branches and utility lines just east of Ogdensburg, including Flackville and Heuvelton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of wintry precipitation impacted portions of eastern New York on the 6th and 7th. The first was a round of light to moderate snow after midnight on 6th to around sunrise, which resulted in one to five inches of snow along and north of the I-88/I-90 corridor. Then, periods of freezing rain occurred from the afternoon of the 6th into the morning of the 7th mainly along and north of I-90. Reports of a quarter to a half an inch of ice accretion were received in the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks into Washington County. The freezing rain quickly turned to heavy snow during the afternoon of the 7th as a strong low pressure system passed south of the area. This round of snow resulted in a quick 6-12 inches of accumulation in the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Lake George Saratoga Region, with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour were common. In the wake of the snowfall, westerly winds gusted to 40-55 miles per hour in the afternoon and evening of the 7th, highest south of I-90.||The weight of the heavy snow and ice on trees along with strong winds resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines. Nearly 90,000 customers lost power, with Saratoga (46,000) and Warren (22,000) Counties being hit the hardest. Over half of Warren County was without power, prompting warming shelters to be opened. Several accidents occurred due to the snowy and icy roads across the southern Adirondacks and Lake George Saratoga Region, including one fatality in Mayfield, and major damage to a fire engine in Chester. A man in Lake Luzerne was injured when he was pierced in the leg while driving a vehicle, and a Thurman Highway Department truck was damaged when a tree fell onto it. Travel was discouraged due to the conditions in Saratoga Springs.

A leading surface boundary sagged from lower Michigan across the Southern Tier and Genesee Valley. Ample moisture pooling evidenced by precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 inches and modest shear generated instability that allowed for thunderstorms to vertically develop into a layer of dry air aloft. This provided the first set of wind damage producing thunderstorms. Subsequent to those, the Lake Ontario lake breeze forced additional thunderstorms that keyed on much higher low level helicity that moved southeastward from Genesee to Ontario counties. These storms developed notable rotation, resulting in one tornado in Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed over Genesee County and tracked|east-southeast from just south of the interchange of the New York|State Thruway and I-490. Scattered tree limb damage consistent|with straight line winds was found along the Oatka Creek upon|following the path of this storm into Monroe County starting|just east of Beulah Road and continuing through Mumford. Damage|became more concentrated upon crossing Wheatland Center Road and|entering Oatka Creek Park. A grassy field was laid down flat in|the opposite direction of the storm motion with tree damage to the|south end of the field indicating rotation with several damaged|trees having broken to the northeast. Farther east, a partially-|flattened corn field with two shallow-rooted uprooted trees along|its southern periphery was coupled with several broken trees along|a hiking path through Oatka Creek Park indicating opposing|directional damage. Scattered damage continued farther east from|Oatka Creek Park along Quaker Road south of Scottsville. A second|more concentrated area of damage was found near the intersection|of Quaker Road and Route 251. Several trees were downed along the|hillside west of Route 251, into Route 251, and on the property at|the southeast corner of Route 251 and Quaker Road. This included|at least three uprooted shallow-rooted trees and one approximately|five foot diameter broken and twisted hardwood tree along with|many downed smaller limbs that indicated a convergent damage path.||Due to the fact that the majority of the path of this storm was|along Oatka Creek and through the Oatka Creek Park and in an area|that is primarily rural, there was no damage to any structures.|Because of this, the National Weather Service use exclusively|tree damage indicators to reach a conclusion of an EF0 strength|and maximum wind speed of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front during the afternoon of the 27th. Two of these storms became quite intense as they tracked from the northern Catskills through the Mid-Hudson Valley and into the Taconics. These storms spawned a brief tornado in a forested area in Greene County, and produced more widespread wind damage in the Amenia, Dover Plains, and Millbrook regions of Dutchess County with numerous downed trees. One fatality occurred in East Fishkill as a tree was downed onto a moving vehicle. More than 4,000 people in Dutchess County and 2,000 people in Ulster County lost power as a result of the storms. The storms also produced a couple of reports of hail up to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Routes 22 was closed due to a downed tree.

Temperatures soared into the 90s throughout much of eastern New York on July 19th, reaching as high as the mid to upper 90s in the Hudson and Mohawk Valleys. The combination of the heat and a humid airmass brought heat indices into the 95 to 105 degree range. The heat prompted the opening of cooling centers and pools, but with reduced capacity due to the ongoing coronavirus pandemic. In addition, a line of thunderstorms moved in from the west during the evening, resulting in a few reports of wind damage in the Schoharie and Mid-Hudson Valleys. Heat indices exceeded 95 degrees again in some of the lower elevations of the Hudson Valley again on July 20th, but were not quite as high as the previous day.

Heat indices exceeded 95 degrees for two consecutive days over portions of the Mid-Hudson Valley on July 22nd and 23rd. In addition, a few severe thunderstorms developed in the area during both afternoons, resulting in several reports of wind damage.

A man in Lake Luzerne was injured when he was pierced in the leg while driving a vehicle, and a Thurman Highway Department truck was damaged when a tree fell onto it. Travel was discouraged due to the conditions in Saratoga Springs.

A strong low pressure system to the west ushered in unseasonably warm and moist air to eastern New York on the 15th. A line of thunderstorms formed along an approaching cold front during the evening hours, resulting in several reports of wind damage in the Mid-Hudson Valley. Damage consisted mainly of downed trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures soared into the 90s throughout much of eastern New York on July 19th, reaching as high as the mid to upper 90s in the Hudson and Mohawk Valleys. The combination of the heat and a humid airmass brought heat indices into the 95 to 105 degree range. The heat prompted the opening of cooling centers and pools, but with reduced capacity due to the ongoing coronavirus pandemic. In addition, a line of thunderstorms moved in from the west during the evening, resulting in a few reports of wind damage in the Schoharie and Mid-Hudson Valleys. Heat indices exceeded 95 degrees again in some of the lower elevations of the Hudson Valley again on July 20th, but were not quite as high as the previous day.

Another hot and humid airmass impacted portions of eastern New York on July 27th and 28th. Heat indices reached 95 to 105 degrees on the 27th in the Hudson and Mohawk Valleys. In addition, a severe thunderstorm caused an isolated wind damage report during the evening. Some areas saw relief from the heat on the 28th, but heat indices exceeded 95 degrees again for portions of the Mid-Hudson Valley. At Poughkeepsie, there were 17 days where the temperature reached 90 degrees during the month of July, tying a record. With an average temperature of 77.8 degrees, July 2020 was the hottest month on record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of wintry precipitation impacted portions of eastern New York on the 6th and 7th. The first was a round of light to moderate snow after midnight on 6th to around sunrise, which resulted in one to five inches of snow along and north of the I-88/I-90 corridor. Then, periods of freezing rain occurred from the afternoon of the 6th into the morning of the 7th mainly along and north of I-90. Reports of a quarter to a half an inch of ice accretion were received in the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks into Washington County. The freezing rain quickly turned to heavy snow during the afternoon of the 7th as a strong low pressure system passed south of the area. This round of snow resulted in a quick 6-12 inches of accumulation in the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Lake George Saratoga Region, with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour were common. In the wake of the snowfall, westerly winds gusted to 40-55 miles per hour in the afternoon and evening of the 7th, highest south of I-90.||The weight of the heavy snow and ice on trees along with strong winds resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines. Nearly 90,000 customers lost power, with Saratoga (46,000) and Warren (22,000) Counties being hit the hardest. Over half of Warren County was without power, prompting warming shelters to be opened. Several accidents occurred due to the snowy and icy roads across the southern Adirondacks and Lake George Saratoga Region, including one fatality in Mayfield, and major damage to a fire engine in Chester. A man in Lake Luzerne was injured when he was pierced in the leg while driving a vehicle, and a Thurman Highway Department truck was damaged when a tree fell onto it. Travel was discouraged due to the conditions in Saratoga Springs.

Severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front during the afternoon of the 27th. Two of these storms became quite intense as they tracked from the northern Catskills through the Mid-Hudson Valley and into the Taconics. These storms spawned a brief tornado in a forested area in Greene County, and produced more widespread wind damage in the Amenia, Dover Plains, and Millbrook regions of Dutchess County with numerous downed trees. One fatality occurred in East Fishkill as a tree was downed onto a moving vehicle. More than 4,000 people in Dutchess County and 2,000 people in Ulster County lost power as a result of the storms. The storms also produced a couple of reports of hail up to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were downed across Shields Road.

The combination of the heat and a humid airmass brought heat indices into the 95 to 105 degree range. The heat prompted the opening of cooling centers and pools, but with reduced capacity due to the ongoing coronavirus pandemic. In addition, a line of thunderstorms moved in from the west during the evening, resulting in a few reports of wind damage in the Schoharie and Mid-Hudson Valleys. Heat indices exceeded 95 degrees again in some of the lower elevations of the Hudson Valley again on July 20th, but were not quite as high as the previous day.

Another hot and humid airmass impacted portions of eastern New York on July 27th and 28th. Heat indices reached 95 to 105 degrees on the 27th in the Hudson and Mohawk Valleys. In addition, a severe thunderstorm caused an isolated wind damage report during the evening. Some areas saw relief from the heat on the 28th, but heat indices exceeded 95 degrees again for portions of the Mid-Hudson Valley. At Poughkeepsie, there were 17 days where the temperature reached 90 degrees during the month of July, tying a record. With an average temperature of 77.8 degrees, July 2020 was the hottest month on record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Building swells and wind waves ahead of Tropical Storm Fay resulted in increasingly dangerous rip current activity Thursday July 9th into Friday July 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A young man drowned and five of his friends had to be rescued from dangerous rip currents in the water off Long Beach Thursday around 7 p.m. The group of six young adults went swimming in the waters off Long Beach and soon had to be rescued by nearby good Samaritans. Lifeguards and surfers in the area rushed to save the group, authorities said.||They were able to pull all six people out of the water, but a 19-year-old from Queens was in cardiac arrest and later died, police said.||According to authorities, the five other swimmers were treated on the scene and released.||Officials said the current was strong near the jetty due to approaching Tropical Storm Fay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front during the afternoon of the 27th. Two of these storms became quite intense as they tracked from the northern Catskills through the Mid-Hudson Valley and into the Taconics. These storms spawned a brief tornado in a forested area in Greene County, and produced more widespread wind damage in the Amenia, Dover Plains, and Millbrook regions of Dutchess County with numerous downed trees. One fatality occurred in East Fishkill as a tree was downed onto a moving vehicle. More than 4,000 people in Dutchess County and 2,000 people in Ulster County lost power as a result of the storms. The storms also produced a couple of reports of hail up to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed in the hamlet of Amenia prompting road closures on Depot Hill Road, Midway Road, and Prospect Avenue.

Multiple rounds of wintry precipitation impacted portions of eastern New York on the 6th and 7th. The first was a round of light to moderate snow after midnight on 6th to around sunrise, which resulted in one to five inches of snow along and north of the I-88/I-90 corridor. Then, periods of freezing rain occurred from the afternoon of the 6th into the morning of the 7th mainly along and north of I-90. Reports of a quarter to a half an inch of ice accretion were received in the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks into Washington County. The freezing rain quickly turned to heavy snow during the afternoon of the 7th as a strong low pressure system passed south of the area. This round of snow resulted in a quick 6-12 inches of accumulation in the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Lake George Saratoga Region, with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour were common. In the wake of the snowfall, westerly winds gusted to 40-55 miles per hour in the afternoon and evening of the 7th, highest south of I-90.||The weight of the heavy snow and ice on trees along with strong winds resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines. Nearly 90,000 customers lost power, with Saratoga (46,000) and Warren (22,000) Counties being hit the hardest. Over half of Warren County was without power, prompting warming shelters to be opened. Several accidents occurred due to the snowy and icy roads across the southern Adirondacks and Lake George Saratoga Region, including one fatality in Mayfield, and major damage to a fire engine in Chester. A man in Lake Luzerne was injured when he was pierced in the leg while driving a vehicle, and a Thurman Highway Department truck was damaged when a tree fell onto it. Travel was discouraged due to the conditions in Saratoga Springs.

Reports of a quarter to a half an inch of ice accretion were received in the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks into Washington County. The freezing rain quickly turned to heavy snow during the afternoon of the 7th as a strong low pressure system passed south of the area. This round of snow resulted in a quick 6-12 inches of accumulation in the Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Lake George Saratoga Region, with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour were common. In the wake of the snowfall, westerly winds gusted to 40-55 miles per hour in the afternoon and evening of the 7th, highest south of I-90.||The weight of the heavy snow and ice on trees along with strong winds resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines. Nearly 90,000 customers lost power, with Saratoga (46,000) and Warren (22,000) Counties being hit the hardest. Over half of Warren County was without power, prompting warming shelters to be opened. Several accidents occurred due to the snowy and icy roads across the southern Adirondacks and Lake George Saratoga Region, including one fatality in Mayfield, and major damage to a fire engine in Chester. A man in Lake Luzerne was injured when he was pierced in the leg while driving a vehicle, and a Thurman Highway Department truck was damaged when a tree fell onto it. Travel was discouraged due to the conditions in Saratoga Springs.
An unstable atmosphere sparked showers and thunderstorms in Central New York during the afternoon and evening of the 27th. Some of these storms became severe and produced mainly tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds brought down trees near Arlington Avenue and Charmouth Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front pushed a very hot air mass into western New York. Temperatures soared to as high as 92 degrees at Dansville. Combined with dewpoints in the low to mid 70s, a very highly unstable environment developed with northwest flow aloft. Surface low pressure tracked southeast across the eastern Lake Ontario region, dragging a trailing cold front southward across areas south of Lake Ontario during the afternoon into the early evening. A line of cumulus clouds over Lake Ontario during the morning hours drifted southward in the mean flow, and with peak daytime heating, blossomed into a cluster of thunderstorms. Abundant instability produced several nice core structures with hail reported to be a large as 2 inches even with a freezing level of over 14,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down with damage to the roof of a barn on Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias moved north along the east coast of the United States on August 3rd and 4th, eventually moving across southeast NY state during the afternoon of August 4th and across the Champlain Valley during the evening hours.||In New York, heavy rainfall was the main impact across the eastern Adirondacks and Champlain Valley with widespread 1.5 to 3 inches of rainfall across Clinton and Essex counties (1-2 inches elsewhere) with a very narrow localized 3-4 inches across the Keene Valley of Essex county and Champlain Valley of Clinton county.||Given very dry antecedent conditions existed prior to the rainfall, minor flooding of poor drainage roads and some swollen streams was the main impact with a few isolated partial road washouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 2-3+ inches was observed across Clinton county, including the following;|Chazy with 3.11 inches, Peru with 3.09 inches and Plattsburgh with 2.51 inches. ||Basically 20 miles either side of line drawn in the locations section.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front during the afternoon of the 27th. Two of these storms became quite intense as they tracked from the northern Catskills through the Mid-Hudson Valley and into the Taconics. These storms spawned a brief tornado in a forested area in Greene County, and produced more widespread wind damage in the Amenia, Dover Plains, and Millbrook regions of Dutchess County with numerous downed trees. One fatality occurred in East Fishkill as a tree was downed onto a moving vehicle. More than 4,000 people in Dutchess County and 2,000 people in Ulster County lost power as a result of the storms. The storms also produced a couple of reports of hail up to half dollar size.

An inverted trough over western New York combined with an eastward propagating lake breeze boundary to support fairly widespread showers and thunderstorms. While the various convective parameters were unimpressive, one particular cell over northern Wyoming county moved northeast and very quickly intensified as it made its way to southern Monroe county. Mesoanalysis identified a ribbon of increased shear and helicity in the axis this storm tracked. This added shear was likely the product of the lake breeze boundary. The storm very quickly generated impressive rotation. While the storm was rotating aloft, the environment was not overly conducive for it to become overly deep. The rotation was also at the apex of a bowing reflectivity pattern rather than being on the southern flank of the storm. It continued to the northeast and crossed Wayne county into the eastern part of Lake Ontario while maintaining some circulation in the low levels and also generating very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed across Mendon Center Road near Carolina Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front started to cross the far western counties of New York, thunderstorms developed in an airmass with little shear but modestly strong instability. Conditions were marginally favorable for waterspouts within cells over Lake Erie, but convective cloud depths were questionable. Upstream reports indicated some waterspouts with the convective activity west of the area over western Lake Erie, and those reports continued into the nearshore area of western New York. As the activity moved into the area, one cell had a very nice lightning core with it.  As it came ashore into Erie County, some rotation developed in the low levels, likely enhanced by friction. This resulted in isolated damage in Orchard Park with no evidence of tornadic activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aluminum poles were sheared off from the bottom at the Orchard Park High School Athletic Field. Additionally, metal benches of 15 ft in length were launched and displaced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A diffuse and weak mid-level trough drifted across the eastern Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening, with a weak inverted trough at the surface extending from the Middle Atlantic states into western New York. Daytime heating resulted in moderate instability, although mid-level lapse rates were poor. Very weak flow through the low and mid-levels provided little to no shear. Scattered thunderstorms developed by midafternoon, with new storm development and subsequent storm motion then driven mainly by outflow boundaries, resulting in slow and chaotic storm motion over the event. A few storms pulsed high enough and long enough to produce severe weather. A few storms also latched onto outflow boundaries and remained nearly stationary or trained for one to two hours, producing isolated flash flooding from northeast Cattaraugus County to the Buffalo Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marble Road and Potter Road were reported to be washed out by law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front pushed a very hot air mass into western New York. Temperatures soared to as high as 92 degrees at Dansville. Combined with dewpoints in the low to mid 70s, a very highly unstable environment developed with northwest flow aloft. Surface low pressure tracked southeast across the eastern Lake Ontario region, dragging a trailing cold front southward across areas south of Lake Ontario during the afternoon into the early evening. A line of cumulus clouds over Lake Ontario during the morning hours drifted southward in the mean flow, and with peak daytime heating, blossomed into a cluster of thunderstorms. Abundant instability produced several nice core structures with hail reported to be a large as 2 inches even with a freezing level of over 14,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 2.06 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A diffuse and weak mid-level trough drifted across the eastern Great Lakes during the afternoon and evening, with a weak inverted trough at the surface extending from the Middle Atlantic states into western New York. Daytime heating resulted in moderate instability, although mid-level lapse rates were poor. Very weak flow through the low and mid-levels provided little to no shear. Scattered thunderstorms developed by midafternoon, with new storm development and subsequent storm motion then driven mainly by outflow boundaries, resulting in slow and chaotic storm motion over the event. A few storms pulsed high enough and long enough to produce severe weather. A few storms also latched onto outflow boundaries and remained nearly stationary or trained for one to two hours, producing isolated flash flooding from northeast Cattaraugus County to the Buffalo Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on Frontenac Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front during the afternoon of the 27th. Two of these storms became quite intense as they tracked from the northern Catskills through the Mid-Hudson Valley and into the Taconics. These storms spawned a brief tornado in a forested area in Greene County, and produced more widespread wind damage in the Amenia, Dover Plains, and Millbrook regions of Dutchess County with numerous downed trees. One fatality occurred in East Fishkill as a tree was downed onto a moving vehicle. More than 4,000 people in Dutchess County and 2,000 people in Ulster County lost power as a result of the storms. The storms also produced a couple of reports of hail up to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed on Pumpkin Lane in the town of Clinton Corners.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and strong moisture advection along with a shortwave tracking north of Lake Ontario helped to generate scattered thunderstorms over southern Ontario to east of Lake Huron in the evening of August 24. Thunderstorms increased in intensity and coverage as they crossed the Niagara Peninsula early in the morning on August 25. This line of storms swept across the Niagara Frontier, including metro Buffalo with wind damage noted south of Lake Ontario. They were out of the area by 6 AM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on Richardson Road in Gowanda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across Long Island and portions of New York City during the early morning hours in association with a surface trough south of the area. With weak steering flow and high moisture content (precipitable water values 2+ on the Upton, NY sounding), this resulted in flash flooding across portions of Nassau and Suffolk Counties. While rainfall totals averaged 1-2 inches across New York City and Long Island, portions of southern Nassau County and southwestern Suffolk County received 3-5 inches of rain. Reports from automated weather observing stations include 3.34 in West Babylon, 3.39 in Freeport, 4.25 in West Gilgo Beach, and 4.46 in Merrick. CoCoRaHS observers reported 3.69 one mile NNE of Wantagh, 4.05 ENE of Copiague, and 5.41 one mile SSW of Massapequa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All lanes were closed in both directions at Exit 5 on the Wantagh State Parkway in Wantagh due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias moved north along the east coast of the United States on August 3rd and 4th, eventually moving across southeast NY state during the afternoon of August 4th and across the Champlain Valley during the evening hours.||In New York, heavy rainfall was the main impact across the eastern Adirondacks and Champlain Valley with widespread 1.5 to 3 inches of rainfall across Clinton and Essex counties (1-2 inches elsewhere) with a very narrow localized 3-4 inches across the Keene Valley of Essex county and Champlain Valley of Clinton county.||Given very dry antecedent conditions existed prior to the rainfall, minor flooding of poor drainage roads and some swollen streams was the main impact with a few isolated partial road washouts.

While rainfall totals averaged 1-2 inches across New York City and Long Island, portions of southern Nassau County and southwestern Suffolk County received 3-5 inches of rain. Reports from automated weather observing stations include 3.34 in West Babylon, 3.39 in Freeport, 4.25 in West Gilgo Beach, and 4.46 in Merrick. CoCoRaHS observers reported 3.69 one mile NNE of Wantagh, 4.05 ENE of Copiague, and 5.41 one mile SSW of Massapequa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous water rescues were reported and several cars were stranded on Sunrise Highway in Massapequa due to flooding near the LIRR underpass and Sunrise Mall. In addition, Massapequa Avenue was closed in front of Fairfield Elementary School due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front pushed a very hot air mass into western New York. Temperatures soared to as high as 92 degrees at Dansville. Combined with dewpoints in the low to mid 70s, a very highly unstable environment developed with northwest flow aloft. Surface low pressure tracked southeast across the eastern Lake Ontario region, dragging a trailing cold front southward across areas south of Lake Ontario during the afternoon into the early evening. A line of cumulus clouds over Lake Ontario during the morning hours drifted southward in the mean flow, and with peak daytime heating, blossomed into a cluster of thunderstorms. Abundant instability produced several nice core structures with hail reported to be a large as 2 inches even with a freezing level of over 14,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down onto Mendon Center Road and Canfield Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front during the afternoon of the 27th. Two of these storms became quite intense as they tracked from the northern Catskills through the Mid-Hudson Valley and into the Taconics. These storms spawned a brief tornado in a forested area in Greene County, and produced more widespread wind damage in the Amenia, Dover Plains, and Millbrook regions of Dutchess County with numerous downed trees. One fatality occurred in East Fishkill as a tree was downed onto a moving vehicle. More than 4,000 people in Dutchess County and 2,000 people in Ulster County lost power as a result of the storms. The storms also produced a couple of reports of hail up to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was sheared off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along the Middle Atlantic coast on Wednesday December 16, 2020. The low deepened as it tracked northward towards Long Island, NY through the night on December 16, 2020.  Arctic high pressure situated over southeast Canada provided a cold and dry air mass ahead of the low supporting moderate to locally heavy snowfall at the onset. The low pressure ultimately tracked from near Atlantic City, NJ to about 50 miles south of Fire Island, NY before sliding offshore Thursday December 17, 2020. This track led to warmer air aloft moving over the NYC metro and Long Island, changing the snow to a mixture of sleet and rain. Surface temperatures briefly warmed into the upper 30s across central and eastern Long Island early on December 17, 2020, changing the precipitation to plain rain. Once the low began moving away from Long Island, temperatures fell significantly below freezing with snow returning as the event ended. Any snowmelt and standing water refroze on Long Island. Any mixed precipitation was brief in the NYC metro and predominately snow fell across the Lower Hudson Valley. The storm significantly impacted travel during the evening commute on Wednesday December 16, 2020 and the morning commute on Thursday December 17, 2020. Many accidents resulted from the snow and icy conditions. Snowfall rates ranged from 1 to 2 inches per hour Wednesday evening. Total snowfall ranged from around 4 inches across coastal southeast Long Island to as much as 14 inches in Orange County.  6 to 12 inches of snow fell in the NYC metro. Strong wind gusts also occurred with the storm, 35 to 60 mph, with some coastal stations reporting gusts as high as 74 mph on Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Official NWS Observation at Central Park, NY was 10.5 inches of snow. The Central Park ASOS measured a 40 mph wind gust at 5:51 am on December 17, 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Water Vapor Imagery showed a well-defined trough cutting across the Ottawa Valley. The surface low was positioned in the vicinity of James Bay. This surface low was 996 hPa and remained steady state through most of the event. Temperatures over the lake were near -9C at 850 hPa but warmed to around -3 or -4C by the end of the event. A heavy band of lake effect snow developed east of Lake Ontario with favorable instability and moisture within the dendritic snow growth zone. Plenty of over-lake instability was in place with lake equilibrium levels rising above 15,000 ft. This amount of extreme instability resulted in some thunder and lightning. Another trough dropped across Lake Ontario during the event, pushing the lake snows southward toward the southeast end of the lake. Selected snowfall totals for the event included 13 inches in Constableville, 8.2 inches near Redfield, 8 inches in Highmarket, and 7.8 inches in Osceola.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer measured 7.6 inches of snow 1 mile east of Searingtown, NY. An NWS Employee in East Meadow measured 6.2 inches of snow. Trained spotters and the public reported 5 to 8 inches of snow. Much of this snow fell before a brief changeover to a mix of rain and sleet. Any snow melt and standing water quickly refroze during the Thursday December 17, 2020 commute as temperatures fell below freezing creating hazardous travel. Additional light snow accumulation also occurred during the morning commute. The Farmingdale Airport ASOS measured a 49 mph wind gust at 7:53 am on December 17, 2020. The Wantagh New York Mesonet station measured a 48 mph wind gust at 6:30 am on December 17, 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted across western New York, laying out from the Eastern Lake Ontario region down toward New York City by the early afternoon. Due to an increase in storm relative helicity along the warm front, rotating thunderstorms resulted. The amount of time the warm front lingered over the area was short, however, as with this initial position, only a few cells developed to the east of Lake Ontario before the boundary and threat for additional severe weather moved to the northeast of our region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed across Centreville Road.

Surface temperatures briefly warmed into the upper 30s across central and eastern Long Island early on December 17, 2020, changing the precipitation to plain rain. Once the low began moving away from Long Island, temperatures fell significantly below freezing with snow returning as the event ended. Any snowmelt and standing water refroze on Long Island. Any mixed precipitation was brief in the NYC metro and predominately snow fell across the Lower Hudson Valley. The storm significantly impacted travel during the evening commute on Wednesday December 16, 2020 and the morning commute on Thursday December 17, 2020. Many accidents resulted from the snow and icy conditions. Snowfall rates ranged from 1 to 2 inches per hour Wednesday evening. Total snowfall ranged from around 4 inches across coastal southeast Long Island to as much as 14 inches in Orange County.  6 to 12 inches of snow fell in the NYC metro. Strong wind gusts also occurred with the storm, 35 to 60 mph, with some coastal stations reporting gusts as high as 74 mph on Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observers and an NWS Employee reported 5 to 7 inches of snow. The snow changed to a brief mix of rain and sleet, then predominately rain during the early morning hours on December 17, 2020. The rain then changed back to snow during the morning commute. Any snow melt and standing water quickly refroze during the commute as temperatures fell below freezing creating hazardous travel. Additional light snow accumulation also occurred during the morning commute. The Southold New York Mesonet station measure a 56 mph wind gust at 2:05 am on December 17, 2020.  Winds gusted to 48 mph at the nearby Westhampton Beach Airport ASOS at 9:53 am on December 17, 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering drought conditions from an abnormally dry summer continued into the month of November for northern Herkimer and the far western portion of Hamilton county. These areas were in D2, or severe drought, until the November 17 update downgraded these areas to D1, or moderate drought. Precipitation totals of 3 to 5 inches fell across northern Hamilton and Herkimer counties during the entire month of November, allowing the severe drought to be downgraded during the middle part of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain and embedded thunderstorms moved through south Central and Eastern New York on the 4th associated with Tropical Storm Isaias. Widespread rainfall affected the area with locally heavy downpours causing isolated flash flooding in Sullivan county. Rainfall estimates were in the 3 to 5 inch range during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Floodwaters inundated a bridge across South Maplewood Road. Scouring and damage of the road surface was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 50 to 55 knot low level jet was moving across western New York during the morning hours as a pre-frontal surface trough was moving through. A 30-40 mile wide swath of steady light rain preceded the surface feature. While there were no visible convective elements within the swath of rain, there was embedded convection just prior to the rain moving through, and there was impressive elevated instability present. The rain temporarily stabilized the air mass to the point where the stronger winds from aloft could not mix to the surface, but as the rain tapered off, winds quickly ramped up with gusts of 40-50 mph. Initial wind gusts over Niagara County were as expected, however just as the rain ended further to the south and east, gusts of 55-60 mph were experienced with damage. The convectively-enhanced winds brought down some trees and caused power outages, mainly across the Genesee Valley and especially the Southern Tier. The radar did show a wavy line of showers moving through the Genesee valley at the time of the highest winds, with the damaging winds coming immediately in the wake of the steady rain across the Southern Tier. This was likely convectively-enhanced wind damage given its proximity to the wavy line of showers and also because winds quickly dropped back to gusts of 30-35 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down in East Rochester via Twitter.

A complex evolution of a storm system moving across the area on Christmas Eve allowed for a slow encroachment of cold air into western New York. This allowed for a slow changeover from rain to snow over far western New York late on Christmas Eve then more than 24 hours later farther east in the area. This allowed for heavy snow accumulation solely in areas west of US Highway 219 with little or no snow east of there from the synoptic system. As colder air wrapped in, lake effect snows gradually increased late on Christmas Day east of Lake Erie and on 12/26 east of Lake Ontario. These dominant bands wobbled in and out of the Buffalo and Watertown areas, resulting in heavy snow accumulations in the urban areas. Selected storm totals from the synoptic portion of the snowfall included 12 inches in Mayville, 9.5 inches in Dunkirk, 10 inches in Perrysburg, and 13.8 inches in Hamburg. Selected lake effect snow totals included 18.4 inches at the Buffalo Airport, 20 inches in Lancaster, 22.3 inches in Alden, 19 inches in Croghan, 15.2 inches in Redfield, 17.4 inches in Watertown, 7.5 inches in Warsaw, 16 inches in Batavia, 21 inches in Elma Center, 16 inches in Dunkirk, and 15.2 inches in Perrysburg.

A hot and humid airmass was in place, providing enough energy for scattered thunderstorms to develop across the region. The hardest hit area was in the town of Whitehall, New York which saw repeated rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours of August 24th. An impressive 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across the far northeast portions of Washington County, resulting in 8 flooded roadways, 21 flooded structures, 1 water rescue, several cars partially submerged and the Whitehall Junior-Senior High School being significantly damaged by the flood waters. The school superintendent estimated that it would cost 16 million dollars to repair the damages from the flood waters. Overall, 40 homes and 13 businesses or non-profit organizations sustained damage totaling 1.5 million dollars. There was also about 1,200 power outages as a result of the storms in Washington County. A Physical Disaster Declaration was declared for Washington County as a result of the storms. There was also one isolated report of trees down in Albany county from a separate storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed onto wires on Gilmore Street and Potter Terrace. Time was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place across the region. This resulted in scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening of August 10th. Some of these storms became severe, with one storm producing an EF1 tornado in Washington County. The storms also produced flash flooding in Ulster County. Heat indices reached the 95 to 100 degree range across the advisory area on Monday, August 10th with heat indices of the same magnitude subdued further south during the afternoon hours of August 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted at the intersection of Warnick and Callaway Streets.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 51 mph was recorded at the Stewart Airport AWOS at 420pm LST, 50 mph at the NYC mesonet station in Warwick at 110 pm LST, and a 45 mph wind gust at the Montgomery Airport AWOS at 346 pm LST. These type of winds and saturated grounds across the county resulted in numerous trees and tree limbs being downed, power outages, damages to homes and automobiles, and disruption to transit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying low pressure system advanced through the western Great Lakes and eventually spun up toward James Bay. This pushed a cold front across the area during the latter half of November 15. Along the cold front, shallow convection developed with widespread non-severe hail and widespread wind gusts over 60 mph. In the wake of the front, a second and longer-lasting period of non-convective high winds persisted through much of the evening and into the overnight east of Lake Ontario. Widespread damage was reported from both the thunderstorm winds and non-thunderstorm winds. Lake Erie topped 11.13 feet at Buffalo due to the first seiche with the frontal boundary thunderstorms then secondarily topped 10.34 feet with the non-convective wind increase in the evening. This resulted in flooding and some evacuations in Angola, Hamburg, and the Old First Ward in Buffalo. Selected peak wind gusts during the afternoon and evening include 60 mph at Wellsville, 69 mph in Fredonia, 69 mph at the Buffalo Coast Guard, 67 mph at the Buffalo Niagara International Airport, 58 mph in Batavia, 58 mph at Fort Drum, 60 mph in Brockport, 62 mph at Niagara Falls Airport, and 59 mph in Warsaw.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance in close proximity to a departing warm front triggered numerous severe thunderstorms across Southeastern New York. These storms produced a tornado in Montgomery, NY in Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EF1 Tornado in Montgomery, NY. Maximum path length was 600 yards and a path length of 2.6 miles.||A National Weather Service storm survey team confirmed an EF1 |tornado in Montgomery, Orange County, NY. The tornado likely |touched down on the northwest side of the Orange County Airport |near the Wallkill River. Straight line winds on the southern flank|of the tornado likely caused tree damage at the Intersection of |Route 211 and Canning Road as well as minor structural damage |noted to two buildings on the airport. A temporary meteorological |station, installed on the airfield to support this weekend's |airshow, measured sustained winds of 69 mph with a peak gust of 82|mph before the communications went out. ||The tornado moved east from near the river into the village of |Montgomery, where damage was extensive along Weaver Street, as |well as Chandler Lane. The tornado then continued eastward across |Beaver Dam Road to Old Nealytown Road where it ended. The bulk of |the damage consisted of large snapped and uprooted trees.

A complex storm system moved from the Lower Mississippi valley to lower Ontario during this time period. Warm and moist air surged northward from the Gulf of Mexico bringing rain and temperatures in the lower 50s. Rainfall on the order of 2 to 4 inches accompanied the storm system. The warmer air and windy conditions rapidly melted a substantial snowpack of 20 to 30 inches in the region. This, coupled with the heavy rainfall, contributed to rapid stream rises resulting in flash flooding, followed by flooding of the main river channels on Christmas Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than a foot of water was rushing down Wagner Avenue. Evacuations were being initiated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias tracked northeast from the eastern Carolinas across the mid-Hudson Valley and into New England. The center of the storm passed close to Albany on August 4th. This storm brought high winds and heavy rain to the region through the period. Heaviest rainfall occurred across the eastern Catskills, into the Capital District and up into the Lake George Saratoga Region, with the highest amounts of 5 to 7 inches in Greene county. Tropical storm force winds occurred mainly south and east of the Capital District with wind damage reports scattered across much of the area. Power outages also occurred as a result of the high winds, especially across Dutchess county with over 50,000 reported outages. Governor Andrew Cuomo declared a State Disaster Declaration for several counties in southern New York, including Dutchess county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed on Blenor Ave in the town of Hudson Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying low pressure system advanced through the western Great Lakes and eventually spun up toward James Bay. This pushed a cold front across the area during the latter half of November 15. Along the cold front, shallow convection developed with widespread non-severe hail and widespread wind gusts over 60 mph. In the wake of the front, a second and longer-lasting period of non-convective high winds persisted through much of the evening and into the overnight east of Lake Ontario. Widespread damage was reported from both the thunderstorm winds and non-thunderstorm winds. Lake Erie topped 11.13 feet at Buffalo due to the first seiche with the frontal boundary thunderstorms then secondarily topped 10.34 feet with the non-convective wind increase in the evening. This resulted in flooding and some evacuations in Angola, Hamburg, and the Old First Ward in Buffalo. Selected peak wind gusts during the afternoon and evening include 60 mph at Wellsville, 69 mph in Fredonia, 69 mph at the Buffalo Coast Guard, 67 mph at the Buffalo Niagara International Airport, 58 mph in Batavia, 58 mph at Fort Drum, 60 mph in Brockport, 62 mph at Niagara Falls Airport, and 59 mph in Warsaw.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place, providing enough energy for scattered thunderstorms to develop across the region. The hardest hit area was in the town of Whitehall, New York which saw repeated rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours of August 24th. An impressive 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across the far northeast portions of Washington County, resulting in 8 flooded roadways, 21 flooded structures, 1 water rescue, several cars partially submerged and the Whitehall Junior-Senior High School being significantly damaged by the flood waters. The school superintendent estimated that it would cost 16 million dollars to repair the damages from the flood waters. Overall, 40 homes and 13 businesses or non-profit organizations sustained damage totaling 1.5 million dollars. There was also about 1,200 power outages as a result of the storms in Washington County. A Physical Disaster Declaration was declared for Washington County as a result of the storms. There was also one isolated report of trees down in Albany county from a separate storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous snapped and downed trees near Skenesborough Drive in Whitehall. A house nearby also had roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place across the region. This resulted in scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening of August 10th. Some of these storms became severe, with one storm producing an EF1 tornado in Washington County. The storms also produced flash flooding in Ulster County. Heat indices reached the 95 to 100 degree range across the advisory area on Monday, August 10th with heat indices of the same magnitude subdued further south during the afternoon hours of August 11th.

Tropical Storm Isaias tracked northeast from the eastern Carolinas across the mid-Hudson Valley and into New England. The center of the storm passed close to Albany on August 4th. This storm brought high winds and heavy rain to the region through the period. Heaviest rainfall occurred across the eastern Catskills, into the Capital District and up into the Lake George Saratoga Region, with the highest amounts of 5 to 7 inches in Greene county. Tropical storm force winds occurred mainly south and east of the Capital District with wind damage reports scattered across much of the area. Power outages also occurred as a result of the high winds, especially across Dutchess county with over 50,000 reported outages. Governor Andrew Cuomo declared a State Disaster Declaration for several counties in southern New York, including Dutchess county.

Severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front during the afternoon of the 27th. Two of these storms became quite intense as they tracked from the northern Catskills through the Mid-Hudson Valley and into the Taconics. These storms spawned a brief tornado in a forested area in Greene County, and produced more widespread wind damage in the Amenia, Dover Plains, and Millbrook regions of Dutchess County with numerous downed trees. One fatality occurred in East Fishkill as a tree was downed onto a moving vehicle. More than 4,000 people in Dutchess County and 2,000 people in Ulster County lost power as a result of the storms. The storms also produced a couple of reports of hail up to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service confirmed a brief tornado from an eyewitness report of about 15 trees with their tops torn off in a forested area on a hilltop at about 2400 feet elevation. This report aligned with strong rotation evident on Doppler radar imagery. The maximum estimated winds were 90 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system moved from the Lower Mississippi valley to lower Ontario during this time period. Warm and moist air surged northward from the Gulf of Mexico bringing rain and temperatures in the lower 50s. Rainfall on the order of 2 to 4 inches accompanied the storm system. The warmer air and windy conditions rapidly melted a substantial snowpack of 20 to 30 inches in the region. This, coupled with the heavy rainfall, contributed to rapid stream rises resulting in flash flooding, followed by flooding of the main river channels on Christmas Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Depot Street flooded and closed at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system moved from the Lower Mississippi valley to lower Ontario during this time period. Warm and moist air surged northward from the Gulf of Mexico bringing rain and temperatures in the lower 50s. Rainfall on the order of 2 to 4 inches accompanied the storm system. The warmer air and windy conditions rapidly melted a substantial snowpack of 20 to 30 inches in the region. This, coupled with the heavy rainfall, contributed to rapid stream rises resulting in flash flooding, followed by flooding of the main river channels on Christmas Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding of creeks and streams throughout Endicott and Vestal, particularly along Vestal Road and Prentice Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place across the region. This resulted in scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening of August 10th. Some of these storms became severe, with one storm producing an EF1 tornado in Washington County. The storms also produced flash flooding in Ulster County. Heat indices reached the 95 to 100 degree range across the advisory area on Monday, August 10th with heat indices of the same magnitude subdued further south during the afternoon hours of August 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed onto a roadway.

A complex evolution of a storm system moving across the area on Christmas Eve allowed for a slow encroachment of cold air into western New York. This allowed for a slow changeover from rain to snow over far western New York late on Christmas Eve then more than 24 hours later farther east in the area. This allowed for heavy snow accumulation solely in areas west of US Highway 219 with little or no snow east of there from the synoptic system. As colder air wrapped in, lake effect snows gradually increased late on Christmas Day east of Lake Erie and on 12/26 east of Lake Ontario. These dominant bands wobbled in and out of the Buffalo and Watertown areas, resulting in heavy snow accumulations in the urban areas. Selected storm totals from the synoptic portion of the snowfall included 12 inches in Mayville, 9.5 inches in Dunkirk, 10 inches in Perrysburg, and 13.8 inches in Hamburg. Selected lake effect snow totals included 18.4 inches at the Buffalo Airport, 20 inches in Lancaster, 22.3 inches in Alden, 19 inches in Croghan, 15.2 inches in Redfield, 17.4 inches in Watertown, 7.5 inches in Warsaw, 16 inches in Batavia, 21 inches in Elma Center, 16 inches in Dunkirk, and 15.2 inches in Perrysburg.

Tropical Storm Isaias tracked northeast from the eastern Carolinas across the mid-Hudson Valley and into New England. The center of the storm passed close to Albany on August 4th. This storm brought high winds and heavy rain to the region through the period. Heaviest rainfall occurred across the eastern Catskills, into the Capital District and up into the Lake George Saratoga Region, with the highest amounts of 5 to 7 inches in Greene county. Tropical storm force winds occurred mainly south and east of the Capital District with wind damage reports scattered across much of the area. Power outages also occurred as a result of the high winds, especially across Dutchess county with over 50,000 reported outages. Governor Andrew Cuomo declared a State Disaster Declaration for several counties in southern New York, including Dutchess county.

Selected storm totals from the synoptic portion of the snowfall included 12 inches in Mayville, 9.5 inches in Dunkirk, 10 inches in Perrysburg, and 13.8 inches in Hamburg. Selected lake effect snow totals included 18.4 inches at the Buffalo Airport, 20 inches in Lancaster, 22.3 inches in Alden, 19 inches in Croghan, 15.2 inches in Redfield, 17.4 inches in Watertown, 7.5 inches in Warsaw, 16 inches in Batavia, 21 inches in Elma Center, 16 inches in Dunkirk, and 15.2 inches in Perrysburg.

Lake effect snow developed southeast of the lower Great Lakes on west-northwest flow. While already in cold advection behind an initial cold front, a secondary cold front dropped southeast, hanging up along Lake Ontario. This allowed for the band to become stationary over southern Oswego County for roughly 5 hours as this front lingered overhead. Reports of 9-13��� were widespread just to the north of Oneida Lake in Oswego County from this band. Selected snow totals included 13 inches in Sand Ridge, 8 inches in Fulton, 12 inches in Central Square, and 9 inches in Phoenix.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place, providing enough energy for scattered thunderstorms to develop across the region. The hardest hit area was in the town of Whitehall, New York which saw repeated rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours of August 24th. An impressive 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across the far northeast portions of Washington County, resulting in 8 flooded roadways, 21 flooded structures, 1 water rescue, several cars partially submerged and the Whitehall Junior-Senior High School being significantly damaged by the flood waters. The school superintendent estimated that it would cost 16 million dollars to repair the damages from the flood waters. Overall, 40 homes and 13 businesses or non-profit organizations sustained damage totaling 1.5 million dollars. There was also about 1,200 power outages as a result of the storms in Washington County. A Physical Disaster Declaration was declared for Washington County as a result of the storms. There was also one isolated report of trees down in Albany county from a separate storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed on Skenesborough Drive in the front of Skeneborough EMS Building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South to southwest winds ahead of a slowly crossing cold front, had built surf to around 3 ft and a moderate risk for rip current development on Tuesday August 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per East Hampton PD, The body of Byron Dong Ha Kim was found Thursday in the Atlantic Ocean off Amagansett, about a half-mile east of where he was last seen swimming with friends near a Napeague resort at midday Tuesday. He was 23 and lived in Brooklyn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying low pressure system advanced through the western Great Lakes and eventually spun up toward James Bay. This pushed a cold front across the area during the latter half of November 15. Along the cold front, shallow convection developed with widespread non-severe hail and widespread wind gusts over 60 mph. In the wake of the front, a second and longer-lasting period of non-convective high winds persisted through much of the evening and into the overnight east of Lake Ontario. Widespread damage was reported from both the thunderstorm winds and non-thunderstorm winds. Lake Erie topped 11.13 feet at Buffalo due to the first seiche with the frontal boundary thunderstorms then secondarily topped 10.34 feet with the non-convective wind increase in the evening. This resulted in flooding and some evacuations in Angola, Hamburg, and the Old First Ward in Buffalo. Selected peak wind gusts during the afternoon and evening include 60 mph at Wellsville, 69 mph in Fredonia, 69 mph at the Buffalo Coast Guard, 67 mph at the Buffalo Niagara International Airport, 58 mph in Batavia, 58 mph at Fort Drum, 60 mph in Brockport, 62 mph at Niagara Falls Airport, and 59 mph in Warsaw.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place across the region. This resulted in scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening of August 10th. Some of these storms became severe, with one storm producing an EF1 tornado in Washington County. The storms also produced flash flooding in Ulster County. Heat indices reached the 95 to 100 degree range across the advisory area on Monday, August 10th with heat indices of the same magnitude subdued further south during the afternoon hours of August 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front during the afternoon of the 27th. Two of these storms became quite intense as they tracked from the northern Catskills through the Mid-Hudson Valley and into the Taconics. These storms spawned a brief tornado in a forested area in Greene County, and produced more widespread wind damage in the Amenia, Dover Plains, and Millbrook regions of Dutchess County with numerous downed trees. One fatality occurred in East Fishkill as a tree was downed onto a moving vehicle. More than 4,000 people in Dutchess County and 2,000 people in Ulster County lost power as a result of the storms. The storms also produced a couple of reports of hail up to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed across the northbound lanes of the Taconic State Parkway at mile 43.4. The tree fell on a vehicle, killing a 73-year old male passenger.

A strong cold front sparked scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon of August 25th. A few of these storms became severe and resulted in downed trees and wires across Saratoga and Dutchess counties. The New York Mesonet site in Beacon recorded a wind gust of 55mph associated with a severe thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and wires downed along Park Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias tracked northeast from the eastern Carolinas across the mid-Hudson Valley and into New England. The center of the storm passed close to Albany on August 4th. This storm brought high winds and heavy rain to the region through the period. Heaviest rainfall occurred across the eastern Catskills, into the Capital District and up into the Lake George Saratoga Region, with the highest amounts of 5 to 7 inches in Greene county. Tropical storm force winds occurred mainly south and east of the Capital District with wind damage reports scattered across much of the area. Power outages also occurred as a result of the high winds, especially across Dutchess county with over 50,000 reported outages. Governor Andrew Cuomo declared a State Disaster Declaration for several counties in southern New York, including Dutchess county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 61 mph was recorded by a WXFLOW system.

Tropical Storm Isaias tracked northeast from the eastern Carolinas across the mid-Hudson Valley and into New England. The center of the storm passed close to Albany on August 4th. This storm brought high winds and heavy rain to the region through the period. Heaviest rainfall occurred across the eastern Catskills, into the Capital District and up into the Lake George Saratoga Region, with the highest amounts of 5 to 7 inches in Greene county. Tropical storm force winds occurred mainly south and east of the Capital District with wind damage reports scattered across much of the area. Power outages also occurred as a result of the high winds, especially across Dutchess county with over 50,000 reported outages. Governor Andrew Cuomo declared a State Disaster Declaration for several counties in southern New York, including Dutchess county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place, providing enough energy for scattered thunderstorms to develop across the region. The hardest hit area was in the town of Whitehall, New York which saw repeated rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours of August 24th. An impressive 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across the far northeast portions of Washington County, resulting in 8 flooded roadways, 21 flooded structures, 1 water rescue, several cars partially submerged and the Whitehall Junior-Senior High School being significantly damaged by the flood waters. The school superintendent estimated that it would cost 16 million dollars to repair the damages from the flood waters. Overall, 40 homes and 13 businesses or non-profit organizations sustained damage totaling 1.5 million dollars. There was also about 1,200 power outages as a result of the storms in Washington County. A Physical Disaster Declaration was declared for Washington County as a result of the storms. There was also one isolated report of trees down in Albany county from a separate storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed on several roads, including Bellamy, North, West, Saunders and Kirtland Streets and on Railroad Avenue. Tree was downed onto a vehicle on Kirtland. Time was estimated by radar.

They later spawned two EF1 tornadoes in the towns of Stillwater and Schaghticoke, with damage to a couple of residences and a high school. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure system brought widespread accumulating snow to our region. The area was mostly just clipped by very heavy snow that fell primarily to our east and southeast Pennsylvania and central New York. While the surface low pressure exited off the east coast in the morning of 12/17, there was a negatively-tilted mid-level trough that passed by following that. This combination of factors led to widespread snow across the area with most areas receiving 3-5 inches, however the heaviest snow was in Allegany County and small portions of adjoining Livingston and Cattaraugus counties where 7-10 inches were common. The most notable center of heavy snow accumulation was in Wellsville where 10 inches was reported.

A complex evolution of a storm system moving across the area on Christmas Eve allowed for a slow encroachment of cold air into western New York. This allowed for a slow changeover from rain to snow over far western New York late on Christmas Eve then more than 24 hours later farther east in the area. This allowed for heavy snow accumulation solely in areas west of US Highway 219 with little or no snow east of there from the synoptic system. As colder air wrapped in, lake effect snows gradually increased late on Christmas Day east of Lake Erie and on 12/26 east of Lake Ontario. These dominant bands wobbled in and out of the Buffalo and Watertown areas, resulting in heavy snow accumulations in the urban areas. Selected storm totals from the synoptic portion of the snowfall included 12 inches in Mayville, 9.5 inches in Dunkirk, 10 inches in Perrysburg, and 13.8 inches in Hamburg. Selected lake effect snow totals included 18.4 inches at the Buffalo Airport, 20 inches in Lancaster, 22.3 inches in Alden, 19 inches in Croghan, 15.2 inches in Redfield, 17.4 inches in Watertown, 7.5 inches in Warsaw, 16 inches in Batavia, 21 inches in Elma Center, 16 inches in Dunkirk, and 15.2 inches in Perrysburg.

A hot and humid airmass was in place, providing enough energy for scattered thunderstorms to develop across the region. The hardest hit area was in the town of Whitehall, New York which saw repeated rounds of heavy rain and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours of August 24th. An impressive 4 to 6 inches of rain fell across the far northeast portions of Washington County, resulting in 8 flooded roadways, 21 flooded structures, 1 water rescue, several cars partially submerged and the Whitehall Junior-Senior High School being significantly damaged by the flood waters. The school superintendent estimated that it would cost 16 million dollars to repair the damages from the flood waters. Overall, 40 homes and 13 businesses or non-profit organizations sustained damage totaling 1.5 million dollars. There was also about 1,200 power outages as a result of the storms in Washington County. A Physical Disaster Declaration was declared for Washington County as a result of the storms. There was also one isolated report of trees down in Albany county from a separate storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires along County Route 7 and Blue Goose Road. Time was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying low pressure system advanced through the western Great Lakes and eventually spun up toward James Bay. This pushed a cold front across the area during the latter half of November 15. Along the cold front, shallow convection developed with widespread non-severe hail and widespread wind gusts over 60 mph. In the wake of the front, a second and longer-lasting period of non-convective high winds persisted through much of the evening and into the overnight east of Lake Ontario. Widespread damage was reported from both the thunderstorm winds and non-thunderstorm winds. Lake Erie topped 11.13 feet at Buffalo due to the first seiche with the frontal boundary thunderstorms then secondarily topped 10.34 feet with the non-convective wind increase in the evening. This resulted in flooding and some evacuations in Angola, Hamburg, and the Old First Ward in Buffalo. Selected peak wind gusts during the afternoon and evening include 60 mph at Wellsville, 69 mph in Fredonia, 69 mph at the Buffalo Coast Guard, 67 mph at the Buffalo Niagara International Airport, 58 mph in Batavia, 58 mph at Fort Drum, 60 mph in Brockport, 62 mph at Niagara Falls Airport, and 59 mph in Warsaw.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down onto Versailles Plank Road new New Jerusalem Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with maximum winds of 100 mph occurred in the town of Stillwater, NY, during the evening of August 29, 2020. Severe damage was done to a mobile home on Mc Dermott Road near the intersection with County Route 75. Several uprooted trees and downed large branches were also noted throughout this area. Witnesses had video of the storm indicating clear rotation and a tornado. This was a brief touchdown with a path length of approximately 0.25 miles and a width of 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place across the region. This resulted in scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening of August 10th. Some of these storms became severe, with one storm producing an EF1 tornado in Washington County. The storms also produced flash flooding in Ulster County. Heat indices reached the 95 to 100 degree range across the advisory area on Monday, August 10th with heat indices of the same magnitude subdued further south during the afternoon hours of August 11th.

Tropical Storm Isaias tracked northeast from the eastern Carolinas across the mid-Hudson Valley and into New England. The center of the storm passed close to Albany on August 4th. This storm brought high winds and heavy rain to the region through the period. Heaviest rainfall occurred across the eastern Catskills, into the Capital District and up into the Lake George Saratoga Region, with the highest amounts of 5 to 7 inches in Greene county. Tropical storm force winds occurred mainly south and east of the Capital District with wind damage reports scattered across much of the area. Power outages also occurred as a result of the high winds, especially across Dutchess county with over 50,000 reported outages. Governor Andrew Cuomo declared a State Disaster Declaration for several counties in southern New York, including Dutchess county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The New York State Mesonet site at Copake, NY measured a maximum wind gust of 50 mph.

An EFO tornado briefly touched down in southwestern Warren County. Sporadic wind damage was noted elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with maximum winds of 110 mph occurred in the town of Schaghticoke, NY, during the evening of August 29, 2020. Significant damage was done to the roof of a house on Verbeck Avenue, and a shed in the backyard of the house was destroyed. Several large trees were uprooted near the house. Farther to the southeast, several trees were downed near a cemetery near the intersection of Routes 40 and 67. Structural damage was also done to the roof of Hoosic Valley Junior Senior High School and elementary school in this area. The tornado continued into Valley Falls, where sporadic tree damage was noted.
